reviews.summary: There have been some papers on getting dense Visual SLAM with neural fields published in the last couple of years. In this work, the authors proposed a set of additions to prior pipelines that get the NN SLAM closer to the traditional approaches concerning the overall framework. Specifically, the authors addressed loop detection, pose graph optimization, bundle adjustment, and collaborative SLAM.
reviews.strengths:
1) Visual-SLAM with neural fields has the huge benefit of providing a dense map of the environment. The topic is relevant to computer vision, robotics, and I believe it fits the NeurIPS scope.
2) The paper is well-written, easy to follow, and well-organized. Related work is OK.
3) Although the technical novelties are relatively limited, in my opinion, the contributions to the pipeline are significant.
reviews.weaknesses:
1) As written above, the technical contributions are limited. The paper focuses more on adding new steps to prior Visual SLAM pipelines.
2) The inclusion of the NetVLAD (not new) as a tool for loop detection looks like a trick. It is not elegant, in the sense that it is a parallel feature extractor that (maybe) could be done using the neural field encoders.
3) Only one dataset and 4 sequences are used, which is small concerning quantity and variety.
4) I would prefer to see more comparisons with previous traditional methods (without neural fields).
5) Figures 5 and 6 are not easily readable.
reviews.questions:
1) I would like the authors to explain what is the motivation for the inclusion of NetVLAD. Can't those descriptors be obtained from the neural field?
2) It is written that a pre-trained NetVLAD was used. It would be important to give more details about this training and how it generalizes to different scenes.
3) Usually, with classical V-SLAM, the bundle adjustment estimates both the pose of the cameras and the 3D map simultaneously. It seems that the authors are doing this alternatively. I would like to see some comments on this.
4) Why are the results for Apartment 2 so bad for the proposed approach? I would like the authors to comment on this.
5) It seems that traditional V-SLAM methods using RGB-D are missing (such as OrbSLAM). I would like to see some comments on this.
6) Maybe I missed something. But it is not clear to me how is the error in Tab. 2 computed.
reviews.limitations: Limitations are adequately addressed.
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
There have been some papers on getting dense Visual SLAM with neural fields published in the last couple of years. In this work, the authors proposed a set of additions to prior pipelines that get the NN SLAM closer to the traditional approaches concerning the overall framework. Specifically, the authors addressed loop detection, pose graph optimization, bundle adjustment, and collaborative SLAM.

Strengths:
1) Visual-SLAM with neural fields has the huge benefit of providing a dense map of the environment. The topic is relevant to computer vision, robotics, and I believe it fits the NeurIPS scope.
2) The paper is well-written, easy to follow, and well-organized. Related work is OK.
3) Although the technical novelties are relatively limited, in my opinion, the contributions to the pipeline are significant.

Weaknesses:
1) As written above, the technical contributions are limited. The paper focuses more on adding new steps to prior Visual SLAM pipelines.
2) The inclusion of the NetVLAD (not new) as a tool for loop detection looks like a trick. It is not elegant, in the sense that it is a parallel feature extractor that (maybe) could be done using the neural field encoders.
3) Only one dataset and 4 sequences are used, which is small concerning quantity and variety.
4) I would prefer to see more comparisons with previous traditional methods (without neural fields).
5) Figures 5 and 6 are not easily readable.

Questions:
1) I would like the authors to explain what is the motivation for the inclusion of NetVLAD. Can't those descriptors be obtained from the neural field?
2) It is written that a pre-trained NetVLAD was used. It would be important to give more details about this training and how it generalizes to different scenes.
3) Usually, with classical V-SLAM, the bundle adjustment estimates both the pose of the cameras and the 3D map simultaneously. It seems that the authors are doing this alternatively. I would like to see some comments on this.
4) Why are the results for Apartment 2 so bad for the proposed approach? I would like the authors to comment on this.
5) It seems that traditional V-SLAM methods using RGB-D are missing (such as OrbSLAM). I would like to see some comments on this.
6) Maybe I missed something. But it is not clear to me how is the error in Tab. 2 computed.

Limitations:
Limitations are adequately addressed.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review: CP-SLAM: Collaborative Neural Point-based SLAM

**Summary:**
This paper introduces CP-SLAM, a collaborative implicit neural simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) system designed to operate with RGB-D image sequences. The authors propose a comprehensive set of additions that bring neural network-based SLAM closer to the capabilities of traditional SLAM approaches. Specifically, CP-SLAM integrates a full front-end and back-end pipeline, which includes essential modules such as robust odometry, learning-based loop detection, sub-map fusion for multi-agent scenarios, and global refinement. This holistic framework aims to overcome common limitations of existing neural SLAM systems, which often lack full SLAM capabilities like loop closure and pose graph optimization, especially in collaborative settings. The core innovation enabling these functionalities is a novel neural point-based 3D scene representation, where each point stores a learnable neural feature and is linked to a keyframe, allowing for flexible map adjustment and fusion.

**Strengths:**
1) **Relevance and Dense Mapping:** Visual SLAM with neural fields offers the significant advantage of providing a dense, high-fidelity map of the environment, unlike many sparse feature-based traditional methods. This capability is highly relevant to current challenges in computer vision and robotics, with direct applications in areas such as autonomous driving, unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) navigation, and virtual/augmented reality (VR/AR), as highlighted in the introduction. The ability of neural fields to encode intricate scene geometry and appearance inherently contributes to superior reconstruction quality, making the topic well within the scope of NeurIPS.
2) **Clarity and Organization:** The paper is notably well-written, easy to follow, and logically organized. The methodology in Section 3, for instance, provides a clear system overview (Figure 1) and systematically breaks down complex components into digestible subsections like "Neural Point based Odometry" and "Distributed-to-Centralized Learning." The contributions are clearly articulated in the introduction, which helps readers grasp the core advancements of CP-SLAM.
3) **Significant Pipeline Contributions:** While the individual technical novelties, such as the neural point representation inspired by Point-NeRF [43], may be considered incremental, their integration into a unified and complete collaborative neural SLAM pipeline represents a significant contribution. The paper effectively addresses the non-trivial challenges of adapting implicit representations for loop closure and collaborative SLAM, as discussed in the introduction, by proposing the neural point-based representation and a two-stage learning strategy. This comprehensive framework bridges the gap between the appealing dense mapping capabilities of neural fields and the robustness and scalability of traditional SLAM systems.

**Weaknesses:**
1) **Limited Technical Contributions:** As noted, the core technical contributions are somewhat limited in terms of proposing entirely new algorithms at a fundamental level. The paper focuses more on integrating and adapting existing concepts (like NetVLAD for loop detection, or bundle adjustment principles for global optimization) within a neural SLAM framework, rather than introducing novel, ground-breaking neural architectures or optimization techniques.
2) **External Loop Detection Component:** The inclusion of NetVLAD [1] as a pre-trained tool for loop detection, while functional, appears to be an somewhat inelegant solution. It introduces a parallel, external feature extractor that is not inherently integrated with the proposed neural point-based representation or its associated MLPs. This design choice deviates from a fully unified neural field approach, where one might expect descriptors to be derived directly from the scene encoding capabilities of the neural field encoders themselves.
3) **Limited Dataset Variety:** The evaluation primarily relies on a single synthetic dataset, Replica [35], comprising 8 collections (4 for single-agent, 4 for two-agent experiments). While the quantity of frames within these collections is substantial (1500-2500 frames per sequence), the overall variety in terms of environment types, textures, lighting conditions, and potential real-world complexities is restricted. Although a small-scale experiment on the TUM-RGBD real-world dataset (Table 3) is included, a more extensive evaluation across a wider range of diverse real-world datasets would significantly bolster the generalizability claims of CP-SLAM.
4) **Insufficient Traditional Baselines:** While the paper does include comparisons with ORB-SLAM3 [4] (a prominent traditional method) in both single-agent and two-agent experiments (Tables 1 and 2), and also CCM-SLAM [33] and Swarm-SLAM [17] for collaborative scenarios, a more comprehensive comparison with a broader set of state-of-the-art traditional RGB-D SLAM systems would be beneficial. Expanding the baselines beyond ORB-SLAM3 to include other robust and widely-used traditional dense or semi-dense RGB-D SLAM methods could provide a clearer picture of CP-SLAM's competitive edge.
5) **Figure Readability:** Figures 5 and 6, which depict two-agent and single-agent trajectories, respectively, are not easily readable. The trajectories often overlap, and the use of small font sizes and potentially similar color schemes for different methods makes it difficult to discern individual paths and compare performance visually. Larger plots, more distinct color palettes, and clearer legends would significantly improve the interpretability of these crucial qualitative results.

**Questions:**
1) **NetVLAD Motivation:** I would appreciate a more detailed explanation regarding the motivation for including the pre-trained NetVLAD model for loop detection (Section 3.2). Specifically, can the authors elaborate on why descriptors for loop closure cannot be effectively obtained directly from the neural field encoders or the learned neural point features within CP-SLAM's unified framework? What specific limitations of the neural point representation or its MLPs necessitated this external component?
2) **Pre-trained NetVLAD Details:** Given that a pre-trained NetVLAD model is used, it would be important to provide more details about its training. What dataset was it pre-trained on, and was it a generic model or one fine-tuned for SLAM applications or similar indoor environments? Understanding this would clarify how well this component is expected to generalize to different and potentially unseen scenes, especially those with varying appearance characteristics.
3) **Bundle Adjustment vs. Alternative Optimization:** In traditional V-SLAM, bundle adjustment typically involves the simultaneous estimation of both camera poses and 3D map points. The paper describes a "global pose graph optimization" (Section 3.4) followed by "global map refinement" where neural point 3D locations are adjusted based on optimized poses. Could the authors comment on the implications of this alternative, sequential optimization strategy compared to a fully joint optimization of both poses and the neural point-based map? Does this sequential approach introduce any trade-offs in terms of accuracy or convergence, particularly for the neural field parameters and point locations?
4) **Apartment 2 Performance Discrepancy:** In Table 1, the results for Apartment-2 show that CP-SLAM (w/) achieves an average ATE RMSE of 1.91 cm, which is notably worse than CCM-SLAM's 0.49 cm. This is a significant outlier compared to CP-SLAM's strong performance in other scenes. Could the authors provide a specific explanation for why the proposed approach performs relatively poorly in Apartment-2 compared to a traditional method like CCM-SLAM? Are there particular characteristics of this scene (e.g., texture, geometric complexity, lighting variations) that pose unique challenges for CP-SLAM's neural representation or its distributed-to-centralized learning strategy?
5) **Broader Traditional RGB-D SLAM Comparisons:** While ORB-SLAM3 is included, the initial thought was that a broader set of traditional RGB-D SLAM methods might be missing. Given that ORB-SLAM3 is indeed a strong baseline for RGB-D, could the authors comment on why other prominent and robust traditional RGB-D SLAM systems (e.g., those using direct methods or different feature extraction strategies, beyond just ORB-SLAM3) were not included for comparison across all relevant experiments?
6) **Table 2 Error Computation:** It is not entirely clear how the error metrics (ATE RMSE, Mean, Median) in Table 2 are computed. While Section 4.2 mentions "origin-aligned manner with the EVO [9] toolbox," could the authors specify the exact alignment method used (e.g., SE(3) or Sim(3) alignment)? This detail is crucial for understanding the exact nature of the reported trajectory accuracy.

**Limitations:**
The authors have adequately addressed the limitations of CP-SLAM in the conclusion. They acknowledge the considerable GPU resource requirements for operating multiple image sequences, the slightly weaker hole-filling ability in unobserved regions due to the surface-centric distribution of neural points, and the potential inaccuracy of rendering-based relative pose computation for large viewpoint changes, which can lead to map drifting. The suggestion for future work on a lightweight system with coarse-to-fine pose estimation is a relevant direction.

**Rating:**
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
There have been some papers on getting dense Visual SLAM with neural fields published in the last couple of years. In this work, the authors proposed a set of additions to prior pipelines that get the NN SLAM closer to the traditional approaches concerning the overall framework. Specifically, the authors addressed loop detection, pose graph optimization, bundle adjustment, and collaborative SLAM.

Strengths:
1) Visual-SLAM with neural fields has the huge benefit of providing a dense map of the environment. The topic is relevant to computer vision, robotics, and I believe it fits the NeurIPS scope.
2) The paper is well-written, easy to follow, and well-organized. Related work is OK.
3) Although the technical novelties are relatively limited, in my opinion, the contributions to the pipeline are significant.

Weaknesses:
1) As written above, the technical contributions are limited. The paper focuses more on adding new steps to prior Visual SLAM pipelines.
2) The inclusion of the NetVLAD (not new) as a tool for loop detection looks like a trick. It is not elegant, in the sense that it is a parallel feature extractor that (maybe) could be done using the neural field encoders.
3) Only one dataset and 4 sequences are used, which is small concerning quantity and variety.
4) I would prefer to see more comparisons with previous traditional methods (without neural fields).
5) Figures 5 and 6 are not easily readable.

Questions:
1) I would like the authors to explain what is the motivation for the inclusion of NetVLAD. Can't those descriptors be obtained from the neural field?
2) It is written that a pre-trained NetVLAD was used. It would be important to give more details about this training and how it generalizes to different scenes.
3) Usually, with classical V-SLAM, the bundle adjustment estimates both the pose of the cameras and the 3D map simultaneously. It seems that the authors are doing this alternatively. I would like to see some comments on this.
4) Why are the results for Apartment 2 so bad for the proposed approach? I would like the authors to comment on this.
5) It seems that traditional V-SLAM methods using RGB-D are missing (such as OrbSLAM). I would like to see some comments on this.
6) Maybe I missed something. But it is not clear to me how is the error in Tab. 2 computed.

Limitations:
Limitations are adequately addressed.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.original_rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

title: Learning Repeatable Speech Embeddings Using An Intra-class Correlation Regularizer

reviews.summary: Starting with the observation that supervised embeddings should vary from one class to another but not be sensitive to variations within a given class, this paper proposes to add an intra-class correlation (ICC) regularization to the contrastive loss in representation learning. This new regularizer is derived from measurement theory and encourages the embeddings to have a low intra-class variance, whereas contrastive losses pushes more the embeddings towards high inter-class variances. After a simulation illustrating the "behaviour" of these losses with respect to inter- and intra-class variance, the authors report good improvements using the proposed ICC regularizer for three different tasks.
reviews.strengths:
- The paper is overall well written, the idea well explained and easy to follow and to understand
  - The ICC regularizer seems to really focus on intra-class variance, i.e. make all occurences of the same class have the same embeddings, where contrastive loss does both at the same time and never really focuses on minimizing the intra-class variance.
  - The method is relatively simple, very well explained and easy to understand. The simulation, comparison with contrastive loss and classification error rate is quite interesting.
  - When the embeddings are constrained, it is indeed useful to focus on minimizing the intra-class variance to separate classes. The explanation and results are convincing.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Missing comparison with other techniques mentioned in the related work (data augmentation, etc.). Is the proposed approach competitive with these methods? Could it be complementary? How does it compare to VICReg [4] for example
  - In the result section, the text mainly lists the results in the tables. Maybe a reformulation would allow more space for example to study the effect of the regularization weight
reviews.questions:
Could that method apply to self-supervised approaches, where the notion of classes is less well defined?

Is it competitive with other methods mentioned at the beginning of the paper? Or could it be complementary?

Are the improvements with that method bigger on smaller datasets or are they independent of the dataset size? Is the method especially suited for some tasks and less for other (e.g. could that be useful for classification, or ASR?)?

minor remark:
  - sec 3.1: "represents the i-th samples" -> sample
reviews.limitations: yes

reviews.text:
Summary:
Starting with the observation that supervised embeddings should vary from one class to another but not be sensitive to variations within a given class, this paper proposes to add an intra-class correlation (ICC) regularization to the contrastive loss in representation learning. This new regularizer is derived from measurement theory, specifically leveraging the concept of repeatability to describe the desired invariance property. It encourages the embeddings to have a low intra-class variance, whereas traditional contrastive losses, such as the GE2E loss, tend to push embeddings more towards high inter-class variances while simultaneously optimizing intra-class variance. After a clear Monte Carlo simulation (Figure 1) illustrating the distinct "behaviour" of these losses with respect to inter- and intra-class variance, the authors report significant improvements using the proposed ICC regularizer for three different speech tasks: text-independent speaker verification, zero-shot voice style conversion, and a clinical application for detecting dysphonic voice.

Strengths:
- The paper is overall well written, the idea well explained and easy to follow and to understand. The introduction clearly lays out the problem of confounding factors in embedding learning and introduces the concept of repeatability from measurement theory in a comprehensible and motivating manner. The connection between ICC as an evaluation metric and its application as a regularizer for embedding quality is intuitively presented and well-justified.
  - The ICC regularizer seems to really focus on intra-class variance, i.e. make all occurrences of the same class have the same embeddings, where contrastive loss does both at the same time and never really focuses on minimizing the intra-class variance. The detailed analysis in Section 3.2, supported by the illustrative contour plots in Figure 1, convincingly demonstrates this distinction between the ICC regularizer and contrastive losses like GE2E. The mathematical analysis of the gradients (Equations 7 and 8) further elucidates why the ICC regularizer inherently prioritizes intra-class variance reduction, especially when inter-class variance is already sufficiently large to separate classes.
  - The method is relatively simple, very well explained and easy to understand. The simulation, comparison with contrastive loss and classification error rate is quite interesting. Section 3.1 provides a clear, step-by-step derivation of the ICC score (Equation 5) and the RICC regularizer (Equation 6), making it straightforward to implement. The Monte Carlo simulation in Section 3.2 is particularly insightful, offering a visual and quantitative comparison of the loss landscapes for both GE2E and the ICC regularizer, which effectively supports the authors' claims about the ICC regularizer's distinct mechanism. The strong alignment of the ICC regularizer's contour with the SVM classification error rate (Figure 1c) on simulated data further reinforces the hypothesis that higher repeatability directly leads to better downstream task performance.
  - When the embeddings are constrained, it is indeed useful to focus on minimizing the intra-class variance to separate classes. The explanation and results are convincing. The L2 normalization of embeddings (Equation 1) implicitly constrains the total variance of the embedding space, making the targeted reduction of intra-class variance by the ICC regularizer particularly effective in improving separability. The empirical results across all three speech tasks (Tables 1, 2, 3, and 4) consistently demonstrate that increased ICC scores, indicating higher repeatability, correlate with significantly improved downstream task performance, such as reduced Equal Error Rate (EER) in speaker verification, higher preference in voice style conversion, and better classification accuracy and generalizability in dysphonic voice detection.

Weaknesses:
- Missing comparison with other techniques mentioned in the related work (data augmentation, etc.). Is the proposed approach competitive with these methods? Could it be complementary? How does it compare to VICReg [4] for example? While the paper acknowledges existing approaches like data augmentation (e.g., "data warping [60, 47]", "data augmentation by GANs [61]") and other novel learning paradigms (e.g., "variance-invariance-covariance regularization [4]" and "invariant risk minimization [2]") in Section 2, a direct empirical comparison against these methods would significantly strengthen the paper's claims regarding the ICC regularizer's unique contribution and overall efficacy. Understanding if ICC offers orthogonal benefits that could be combined with or if it supersedes existing invariance-promoting techniques would be valuable for the community.
  - In the result section, the text mainly lists the results in the tables. Maybe a reformulation would allow more space for example to study the effect of the regularization weight. The results sections (4.1, 4.2, 4.3) effectively present the numerical improvements in EER, preference scores, and accuracy for the respective tasks. However, a deeper discussion of the *implications* of these improvements, such as the practical significance of a ~10% EER reduction for the VGG-M-40 model in speaker verification (Table 1) or how the 61.9% preference in the AB test for voice style conversion (Table 2) translates into user experience, would enrich the narrative. Furthermore, while Appendix B shows the effect of the hyperparameter λ on the combined loss contour, a more detailed ablation study or discussion of its practical tuning and sensitivity in the main results section would be beneficial, moving beyond simply stating it was determined via grid search.

Questions:
Could that method apply to self-supervised approaches, where the notion of classes is less well defined? The authors briefly touch upon this in the "Future Works" section (Section 5) by suggesting "extension to self-supervised methods that use contrastive-style training." However, without explicit class labels, defining "intra-class variance" for ICC becomes a non-trivial challenge. Would "pseudo-labels" or clusters generated by the self-supervised method serve as suitable classes, and how would the ICC regularizer need to be adapted for such scenarios to ensure meaningful repeatability?

Is it competitive with other methods mentioned at the beginning of the paper? Or could it be complementary? Beyond the direct comparisons with various supervised contrastive losses (GE2E, AngleProto, SupCon), it remains unclear how the ICC regularizer performs against or can be combined with other invariant embedding learning strategies, such as those that explicitly enforce equivariance and invariance [42] or invariant risk minimization [2], as discussed in Section 2. A more explicit discussion of its position relative to these broader categories of methods would clarify its unique niche and potential for integration.

Are the improvements with that method bigger on smaller datasets or are they independent of the dataset size? Is the method especially suited for some tasks and less for other (e.g. could that be useful for classification, or ASR?)? The current experiments use relatively large datasets like VoxCeleb 1 & 2 for speaker tasks and SVD/mPower for clinical applications. Investigating the method's performance across varying dataset sizes, particularly smaller ones where intra-class variance might be harder to model, could reveal interesting insights into its robustness and applicability. While the paper focuses on speech tasks, the "Future Works" section (Section 5) indicates potential application to "computer vision and natural language processing." A brief discussion on *why* it might be useful for general classification or Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) tasks beyond the current speaker/voice feature embeddings would be helpful.

minor remark:
  - sec 3.1: "represents the i-th samples" -> sample (This typo appears in the first paragraph of Section 3.1, specifically in the sentence defining `xji`).

Limitations:
Yes, the authors adequately discuss potential negative societal impacts (e.g., amplifying biases in high-stakes applications like healthcare) and acknowledge the need for thorough validation and mitigation strategies.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

**Summary:**
The paper presents a simple but seemingly effective idea to regularize contrastive embedding extractors. Specifically, it introduces the concept of repeatability from measurement theory into deep learning, proposing the use of the intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) as both an evaluation metric and a novel regularizer. The regularizer aims at the intra-class correlation to ensure the repeatability of the embedding, encouraging embeddings from the same class to be consistent despite confounding factors. The effectiveness of this approach is confirmed experimentally for the task of speaker verification, voice style conversion, and dysphonic voice detection, demonstrating that embeddings with higher repeatability lead to improved performance in these downstream applications.

**Strengths:**
The presented idea is well introduced, establishing a clear connection between the concept of repeatability from measurement theory and the desired properties of deep-learned embeddings. The authors clearly define the ICC (Equations 2-5) and explain its interpretation, providing a solid theoretical foundation for its application. The illustration of the ICC regularizer action compared to the GE2E loss in Section 3.2 is particularly insightful. Figure 1 effectively visualizes the distinct optimization landscapes of GE2E loss and the proposed ICC regularizer, clearly demonstrating how the ICC regularizer places a greater emphasis on minimizing intra-class variance once sufficient inter-class separation is achieved, unlike GE2E which continues to push for larger inter-class variance. The accompanying gradient analysis (Equations 7-8) further strengthens this explanation, providing a compelling argument for the ICC regularizer's effectiveness in enhancing repeatability. Moreover, the alignment of the ICC regularizer's contour with the SVM classification error rate (Figure 1c) provides strong evidence that improving repeatability directly benefits downstream tasks. The evaluation is sufficient, covering a diverse range of three speech-related tasks: text-independent speaker verification, zero-shot voice style conversion, and a clinical application for dysphonic voice detection. For speaker verification, the authors rigorously test the ICC regularizer with multiple popular contrastive losses (GE2E, AngleProto, SupCon) and different encoder architectures (VGG-M-40, FastResNet-34), consistently showing performance improvements (e.g., EER decreases by ~10% for VGG-M-40 with GE2E+ICC). The voice style conversion task includes both objective metrics and a subjective AB preference test (where ICC-regularized embeddings were preferred 62% to 38%), which adds significant credibility to the perceived quality improvements. The clinical application for dysphonic voice detection further highlights the practical utility and generalizability of the method, demonstrating improved classification accuracy and model generalizability across different corpora, with a remarkable 30.68% improvement in ICC score compared to the baseline Zhang et al. model (Table 4).

**Weaknesses:**
The introduced regularizer uses 2nd order statistics only, specifically focusing on means and variances (as defined in Equations 3 and 4 for MSB and MSW). This reliance on second-order moments could be a limiting factor on the embedding extractors, as it might not fully capture more complex, higher-order statistical relationships or intricate distributional properties within and between classes. However, on the other hand, for many practical applications, effectively minimizing intra-class variance and maximizing inter-class variance based on these statistics doesn't necessarily need to be required to consider higher-order statistics to achieve substantial performance gains.

**Questions:**
The contrastive loss has a direct relationship to the mutual information, which theoretically aims to maximize the information shared between representations and their augmented views or labels. Given that maximizing mutual information could implicitly encourage features to be invariant to confounding factors (and thus repeatable), one could think that ICC is automatically included in the mutual information when only 2nd order statistics are taken into account. What could be the reason that this doesn't seem to be the case, and an explicit ICC regularizer offers additional benefits? The paper's simulation in Section 3.2 suggests a difference in optimization focus (simultaneous optimization of both variances vs. prioritizing intra-class variance), but a deeper theoretical explanation of why a mutual information objective wouldn't naturally converge to similar repeatability properties, especially considering the per-dimension ICC calculation, would be valuable.

**Limitations:**
A notable limitation is that the introduced regularizer uses 2nd order statistics only, as detailed in Equations 2-4. While effective for the demonstrated tasks, this approach might not fully capture all nuances of embedding quality, particularly for tasks requiring sensitivity to more complex data distributions or higher-order feature relationships. The authors acknowledge this implicitly by suggesting "a more thorough theoretical analysis of the ICC and its properties" as future work, which could explore whether incorporating higher-order statistics or alternative measures of consistency might yield further improvements.

**Rating:**
6: Weak Accept: The paper is technically solid, presenting a novel and well-motivated regularizer for learning repeatable embeddings. The comprehensive experimental evaluation across diverse speech tasks, coupled with insightful analysis of the regularizer's behavior, demonstrates its moderate-to-high impact. The concerns regarding the reliance on 2nd order statistics are acknowledged and do not represent a major impediment to the paper's overall contribution. The work is well-presented, reproducible, and addresses ethical considerations appropriately.

reviews.summary: In this paper, authors propose to use an intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) regularizer as a complimentary component for contrastive loss to increase the repeatability of the supervised speech embedding. Authors conduct simulation for ICC regularizer and contrastive loss (GE2E loss) to explain their similarities and differences in the optimization criteria. Based on the experimental results on the speaker verification, voice style conversion, and a clinical application for detecting dysphonic three tasks, the authors claim that adding an ICC regularizer helps increase the repeatability and further improve the performance on downstream tasks.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper leverages the concept of repeatability and states that higher repeatability can contribute to improved performance in downstream tasks. Authors think about the importance of intra-class variance and the proposed ICC regularizer places greater emphasis on minimizing it to help increase repeatability. reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

**Summary:**
In this paper, authors propose to use an intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) regularizer as a complimentary component for contrastive loss to increase the repeatability of the supervised speech embedding. The core idea is to leverage the concept of repeatability from measurement theory, where it describes the consistency of measurements of the same target under unaltered conditions, and apply this to deep-learned embeddings. The authors highlight that effective embeddings should be sensitive to changes in the target class while remaining invariant to confounding factors, a property directly addressed by high repeatability. Authors conduct simulation for ICC regularizer and contrastive loss (GE2E loss) to explain their similarities and differences in the optimization criteria, demonstrating how the ICC regularizer specifically targets the minimization of intra-class variance. Based on the experimental results on the speaker verification, voice style conversion, and a clinical application for detecting dysphonic three tasks, the authors claim that adding an ICC regularizer helps increase the repeatability and further improve the performance on downstream tasks.

**Strengths:**
1.  This paper leverages the concept of repeatability from measurement theory and explicitly connects it to the quality of deep-learned embeddings, stating that higher repeatability can contribute to improved performance in downstream tasks. This connection is a novel and valuable perspective for representation learning, moving beyond indirect evaluation through downstream task performance. Authors think about the importance of intra-class variance and the proposed ICC regularizer places greater emphasis on minimizing it to help increase repeatability. The simulation results in Section 3.2 clearly explain this intuition, particularly through Figure 1, which visually demonstrates how the ICC regularizer's gradient descent path prioritizes reducing intra-class variance (M SW) over indefinitely increasing inter-class variance (M SB) once clusters are well-separated, unlike the GE2E loss. This analysis effectively supports the claim that the ICC regularizer offers a new perspective for latent representation learning by focusing on intra-class variance reduction.

2.  As a complementary component to contrastive loss, adding the proposed ICC regularizer successfully increases the repeatability of the learned embeddings and shows good performance in the three different speech tasks. For instance, in the text-independent speaker verification (TI-SV) task (Table 1), the ICC score for VGG-M-40 with GE2E loss increased from 0.4494 to 0.5487, leading to a significant ~10% reduction in EER. Similarly, for zero-shot voice style conversion, the AB preference test (Table 2) showed that 61.90% of samples generated with ICC-regularized embeddings were preferred, and objective metrics (Table 3) like speaker similarity and CER also improved. In the clinical application for dysphonic voice detection (Table 4), the proposed method achieved an ICC score of 0.5708, a 30.68% improvement over the baseline, along with better classification accuracy and generalizability across different corpora. The consistent improvements across diverse applications, from speaker identity to voice quality, underscore the broad applicability and effectiveness of the ICC regularizer.

**Weaknesses:**
1.  In Task 1, the performance of SV system is not good though adding the ICC regularizer improves its performance. In this paper, VGG-M-40 is trained on VoxCeleb1 & 2. The best result (3.78% EER) reported in Table 1 for VGG-M-40 with SupCon + ICC is not as good as other commonly used models for SV, especially more recent architectures. For example, the FastResNet-34 model, also used in this paper, achieves a much better EER of 2.16% (SupCon + ICC). In Chung et al. paper, the VGG-M-40 shows much worse performance than Thin-ResNet34 and Fast ResNet34. To make the experimental solid and results and claims more convincible, authors should pick a good baseline instead of using the VGG-M-40 as a primary comparison, or at least provide a more thorough justification for its inclusion given its relatively weaker performance in the current speaker verification landscape.

2.  For better reproducibility, more experiment details should be provided, such as the full training configurations for Task 1. While some details like Adam optimizer, static learning rate of 0.001, and dropout rate of 0.2 are mentioned, specifics like the number of training epochs/steps, total training time, and the exact hardware setup for each model (VGG-M-40 vs. FastResNet-34) are missing. Similarly, for Task 2, while the training procedure refers to Qian et al. [40], explicit confirmation of all hyperparameters used would be beneficial. Providing these granular details would greatly assist other researchers in replicating the reported results.

3.  To have comprehensive evaluation results for Task 1, the evaluation metric minDCF can be used as well. While Table 1 does present minDCF values alongside EER, a more detailed discussion or interpretation of these values, especially in comparison to other benchmarks or previous works, would enrich the evaluation. For instance, analyzing how minDCF improves with the ICC regularizer, similar to the EER discussion, would provide a more complete picture of the performance gains.

4.  Visualization (such as t-SNE or UMAP) can be added as a comparison to see if the extracted speech or speaker embeddings have smaller intra-class variance and greater inter-class separation in the overall embedding space, though their ICC is decreased. Such visualizations would provide intuitive and qualitative evidence to complement the quantitative ICC scores, helping readers visually grasp how the ICC regularizer compacts within-class clusters and separates between-class clusters in the high-dimensional latent space. This would offer a direct visual confirmation of the core mechanism proposed by the authors.

**Questions:**
1.  As this paper mentioned, the ICC regularizer requires hyperparameter fine-tuning for the weighting factor λ in the combined loss function Lcontr + λRICC. Section 4.1 briefly states that "The hyper-parameter is tuned on the development dataset. The EER for subjects in the development dataset are used to determine the optimized hyperparamter." Could the authors provide more specifics on the range of λ values explored during this grid search for each task and model? A more detailed description of the hyperparameter tuning methodology would enhance clarity and reproducibility, perhaps by including the full search space or the specific values that were found to be optimal for each experiment (e.g., λ=0.06 for VGG-M-40 and λ=0.25 for FastResNet-34 in Task 1).

2.  As this paper mentioned in Section 3.2, the ICC regularizer focuses on decreasing intra-class variance. Although the ICC values are increased, do the learned embeddings are more compacted within-class and more separate between-class in the overall embedding space? Authors can use t-SNE or similar dimensionality reduction techniques to visualize them. This would provide visual confirmation that the numerical improvements in ICC scores indeed translate to a more desirable geometric arrangement of embeddings, where samples from the same class form tighter clusters and different classes are more distinctly separated, as hypothesized by the paper's core premise.

**Limitations:**
The authors do not analyze the potential negative societal impact. While a section on "Potential Negative Societal Impact" is present and discusses the risk of amplifying biases in high-stakes applications like healthcare, the analysis could be expanded to include more concrete examples or proposed mitigation strategies specific to the speech tasks discussed. For instance, given the clinical application for dysphonic voice detection, a deeper dive into potential biases related to demographics (e.g., age, gender, accent) or specific medical conditions in the training data, and how these biases might be detected and addressed, would strengthen this section.

**Rating:**
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

Summary:
This paper introduces the concept of repeatability from measurement theory into the domain of representation learning, proposing the Intra-class Correlation Coefficient (ICC) as both an evaluation metric and a novel regularizer. The ICC regularizer is designed to complement traditional contrastive losses, specifically guiding deep neural networks to produce embeddings with higher repeatability by focusing on minimizing intra-class variance. The authors use simulated data and a detailed gradient analysis to demonstrate why the ICC regularizer is more effective at reducing intra-class variance compared to contrastive loss alone, providing a fresh perspective on latent representation learning. The proposed method is thoroughly assessed across three distinct speech tasks: learning speaker embeddings for Text-Independent Speaker Verification (TI-SV), generating speaker embeddings for zero-shot voice style conversion, and deriving voice feature embeddings for a clinical application in dysphonic voice detection. The comprehensive experimental results consistently indicate that integrating the ICC regularizer significantly boosts the repeatability of the learned embeddings, and crucially, these more repeatable embeddings consistently lead to improved performance in their respective downstream tasks.

Strengths:
The paper proposed a novel metric of ICC based on measurement repeatability, which is a significant contribution to the field of representation learning. This explicit connection to established measurement theory, as detailed in Section 1, provides a robust theoretical foundation for evaluating and enforcing a crucial property of embeddings. Unlike many existing methods that only indirectly assess embedding quality through downstream task performance, the ICC offers a direct, quantifiable measure of consistency within a class. Although the term "repeatability" itself might initially be a little confusing in the context of deep learning, the motivation and intuition behind using ICC were exceptionally well explained. The authors' detailed analysis in Section 3.2, particularly the Monte Carlo simulation and gradient discussion presented in Figure 1 and Equations 7-8, clearly illustrates the distinct optimization landscape of the ICC regularizer compared to contrastive losses like GE2E, making the argument for its superior focus on intra-class variance highly compelling. The experimental results on three diverse speaker/speech tasks are convincing, providing strong empirical evidence for the method's effectiveness. For instance, in the TI-SV task, the ICC regularizer consistently improved EER by approximately 10% for the VGG-M-40 model while simultaneously increasing the ICC score (Table 1). Similarly, the zero-shot voice style conversion task showed a clear preference for ICC-regularized embeddings (61.90% vs 38.10% in AB preference tests, Table 2), and the dysphonic voice detection task demonstrated a substantial 30.68% improvement in ICC score and enhanced generalizability across unseen corpora (Table 4). These consistent improvements across varied applications strongly support the paper's central hypothesis.

Weaknesses:
To show the repeatability of speaker embedding for TI-SV task, more controlled experiments may be necessary to prove the effectiveness of ICC loss in addition to the ICC scores. While the paper includes data augmentation with Gaussian noise and varying audio lengths during training, evaluating the learned embeddings under more challenging, controlled testing conditions would further solidify the claim of enhanced repeatability. For example, explicitly adding noise to the testing sample at varying SNR levels, or switching languages of the same speaker (if multi-lingual data were available) as is often tested in the NIST Speaker Recognition Evaluation, would provide stronger evidence of the embeddings' robustness and consistency under real-world confounding factors. The current improvements in EER and ICC scores are promising, but additional stress tests would be invaluable.

For results on dysphonic voice detection in Table 4, it appears that other baseline models were definitely over-trained compared to the proposed model, particularly evidenced by the stark differences between SVD Train and SVD Validation accuracies for methods like Verde et al. (0.8910 vs 0.6274) or Huckvale et al. (0.7290 vs 0.6255). This suggests that while the authors rebuilt and trained these models on their data for a fair comparison of methodology (as stated in lines 334-335 and Appendix C), the observed performance discrepancies might make the comparison of absolute classification accuracy potentially unfair, especially regarding generalizability. The proposed method shows a more consistent performance across training and validation sets (0.7353 vs 0.7289), indicating better generalization. The results in Table 4 may therefore represent an unfair comparison of baseline *performance* due to this apparent overfitting, even if the *training procedure* was consistent.

Questions:
Regarding the TI-SV task, could the authors provide further insights or experiments on how the ICC regularizer impacts embedding robustness under specific types of environmental variations, such as different noise types or reverberation levels, beyond the Gaussian noise used in training? This would help clarify the extent to which "repeatability" translates to "invariance" to different confounding factors in real-world scenarios.

Considering the dysphonic voice detection results in Table 4, could the authors elaborate on the specific training hyperparameters or architectural choices for the rebuilt baseline models that might have led to the observed large discrepancies between in-corpus training and validation accuracies, particularly for Verde et al. and Huckvale et al.? A discussion on strategies employed to mitigate overfitting for the proposed model, which appears to generalize better, would also be beneficial to contextualize the comparison.

Limitations:
The paper lacks a dedicated or sufficiently detailed discussion on potential negative societal impacts. While the authors briefly touch upon bias amplification in high-stakes applications like healthcare (Page 10, "Potential Negative Societal Impact"), a more comprehensive analysis of how these repeatable embeddings might lead to unintended consequences, particularly in sensitive domains such as clinical diagnostics or speaker identification in surveillance, would strengthen the ethical considerations section. Specific examples of how biases in training data could be exacerbated by a focus on "repeatability" and concrete strategies for detection and mitigation would enhance this important aspect of the work.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.questions:
1. In Section 4.2, ''GE2E Loss + ICC Regularizer'' shows superior speech naturalness on subjective evaluations. However, the objective metrics like speaker similarity scores and word error rate (WER) are missing.
2. In Section 4.2, the paper aims at zero-shot voice style conversion, which requires highly generalized speaker embedding. However, the authors train and evaluate the zero-shot voice style conversion on a small dataset called VCTK. I would like to ask if the generalization performance of the proposed method be further improved by using larger training datasets? Additional experiments on larger dataset would make the paper more convincing.
3. Although the audio samples with ICC regularizer is better, after listening to the audio samples in the supplementary materials, I think the audio quality should be further improved for practical scenarios. I would like to ask is this due to the used AutoVC model? Could the authors use the latest SOTA voice conversion model to improve the speech quality?

I would like to raise my scores if the authors have fully addressed my concerns.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

Summary:
This paper introduces the Intra-class Correlation Coefficient (ICC) regularizer, a novel regularization technique that complements the contrastive loss to enhance the repeatability of embeddings. The authors leverage the concept of repeatability from measurement theory, applying it to deep-learned embeddings for both evaluation and training. The manuscript further illustrates, through simulation and gradient analysis, why the ICC regularizer better minimizes the intra-class variance than the contrastive loss alone, offering a fresh and insightful perspective on latent representation learning. The experimental results, spanning three diverse speech tasks—speaker verification, zero-shot voice style conversion, and a clinical application for detecting dysphonic voice—demonstrate that the ICC regularizer significantly enhances the repeatability of learned embeddings. Consequently, embeddings with higher repeatability consistently exhibit superior performance in these downstream tasks, affirming the practical utility of the proposed method.

Strengths:
1.  This paper links the concept of repeatability, derived from measurement theory, to deep-learned embeddings, which is novel and interesting. The application of the Intra-class Correlation Coefficient (ICC), a well-established metric from measurement theory, to directly evaluate and regularize deep-learned embeddings is a significant conceptual contribution. The authors present a clear explanation of how the ICC regularizer effectively reduces intra-class variance compared to the contrastive loss, as visually supported by the distinct contour figures in Figure 1. This analysis offers a novel and valuable perspective on optimizing latent representation learning for consistency and robustness, moving beyond indirect evaluation through downstream task performance.
2.  To enhance the repeatability of the learned embeddings, this paper introduces a novel regularizer called the ICC regularizer, which is used as a complementary component for the contrastive loss. The formulation of the ICC regularizer as `RICC = 1 - ICC(e)` provides a straightforward yet powerful mechanism to guide deep neural networks towards producing embeddings with higher internal consistency. This approach explicitly targets the reduction of intra-class variance, which is crucial for robust and reliable representations in various applications, as highlighted in Section 3.1.
3.  The experimental results indicate that using the ICC regularizer consistently leads to better performance across three distinct downstream tasks. In the text-independent speaker verification (TI-SV) task, the ICC regularizer improved the Equal Error Rate (EER) by approximately 10% for the VGG-M-40 model (e.g., from 4.39% to 3.96% for GE2E + ICC in Table 1) and increased the ICC score, demonstrating enhanced repeatability and verification accuracy. For zero-shot voice style conversion, the ICC-regularized embeddings resulted in a strong subjective preference of 61.90% over non-regularized embeddings (Table 2), alongside improvements in objective metrics like speaker similarity and character error rate (Table 3). Finally, in the clinical application of dysphonic voice detection, the proposed method achieved a notable 30.68% improvement in ICC score (from 0.4368 to 0.5708 in Table 4) and improved classification accuracy and generalizability across different corpora (SVD, MEEI, HUPA), showcasing the broad applicability and tangible benefits of the proposed regularization technique.

Weaknesses:
My main concerns are mainly related to the experiments, particularly regarding the evaluation scope and methodology. Please refer to the following Questions section for details.

Questions:
1.  In Section 4.2, the subjective AB preference test indicates that "GE2E Loss + ICC Regularizer" shows superior speech naturalness. However, the objective metrics provided in Table 3 (Speaker Similarity Score, WER, CER) primarily assess the retention of speaker identity and linguistic content intelligibility. While these are important, objective metrics specifically tailored to quantify "speech naturalness" (e.g., Mel-Cepstral Distortion (MCD), F0 correlation, or other perceptual quality measures) are missing. Including such metrics would provide stronger quantitative evidence to corroborate the subjective findings regarding naturalness.
2.  In Section 4.2, the paper aims at zero-shot voice style conversion, which inherently requires highly generalized speaker embeddings capable of handling a wide range of unseen speakers. However, the authors train and evaluate the zero-shot voice style conversion on a relatively small dataset called VCTK, which comprises 109 speakers. Given the critical need for generalization in zero-shot tasks, I would like to ask if the generalization performance of the proposed method could be further improved by using larger and more diverse training datasets for the voice conversion model itself, beyond just the speaker encoder. Additional experiments on a larger dataset (e.g., LibriSpeech, or larger subsets of VoxCeleb) would make the paper more convincing regarding the robustness and scalability of the proposed method for real-world zero-shot applications.
3.  Although the audio samples generated with the ICC regularizer are subjectively preferred over the baseline, after listening to the audio samples in the supplementary materials, I think the overall audio quality should be further improved to meet the standards required for practical scenarios. I would like to ask if this perceived limitation in audio quality is primarily due to the choice of the AutoVC model, which, while a strong baseline, was introduced in 2019 and might not represent the latest advancements in voice conversion synthesis quality. Could the authors consider integrating the ICC regularizer with a more recent state-of-the-art voice conversion model (e.g., those based on diffusion, VITS, or flow-based architectures) to demonstrate the full potential of the repeatable embeddings in achieving higher fidelity and naturalness in the converted speech?

Limitations:
Some limitations of the work are discussed by the authors, including future directions for applying the ICC regularizer to other domains (computer vision, NLP), extending it to self-supervised methods, and conducting more thorough theoretical analysis. However, while the authors include a dedicated section on "Potential Negative Societal Impact," the review suggests that this important aspect could be more thoroughly integrated into the discussion of limitations, perhaps by outlining specific mitigation strategies or further research directions required for safe deployment, especially given the high-stakes clinical application for detecting dysphonic voice.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper introduces the ICC regularizer, a novel regularization technique that complements the contrastive loss to enhance the repeatability of embeddings. The authors illustrate the reason why the ICC regularizer better minimizes the intra-class variance than the contrastive loss, offering a fresh perspective on latent representation learning. The experimental results demonstrate that the ICC regularizer enhances the repeatability of learned embeddings. Consequently, embeddings with higher repeatability exhibit superior performance in downstream tasks.

Strengths:
1. This paper links the concept of repeatability, derived from measurement theory, to deep-learned embeddings, which is novel and interesting. The authors present an explanation of how the ICC regularizer effectively reduces intra-class variance compared to the contrastive loss, offering a novel perspective on latent representation learning.
2. To enhance the repeatability of the learned embeddings, this paper introduces a novel regularizer called the ICC regularizer, which is used as a regularizer for the contrastive loss.
3. The experimental results indicate that using the ICC regularizer leading to better performance in three downstream tasks .

Weaknesses:
My main concerns are mainly related to the experiments. Please refer to the following Questions section for details.

Questions:
1. In Section 4.2, ''GE2E Loss + ICC Regularizer'' shows superior speech naturalness on subjective evaluations. However, the objective metrics like speaker similarity scores and word error rate (WER) are missing.
2. In Section 4.2, the paper aims at zero-shot voice style conversion, which requires highly generalized speaker embedding. However, the authors train and evaluate the zero-shot voice style conversion on a small dataset called VCTK. I would like to ask if the generalization performance of the proposed method be further improved by using larger training datasets? Additional experiments on larger dataset would make the paper more convincing.
3. Although the audio samples with ICC regularizer is better, after listening to the audio samples in the supplementary materials, I think the audio quality should be further improved for practical scenarios. I would like to ask is this due to the used AutoVC model? Could the authors use the latest SOTA voice conversion model to improve the speech quality?

I would like to raise my scores if the authors have fully addressed my concerns.

Limitations:
Some limitations of the work are discussed. The potential negative societal impact of the work is not addressed.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes to use a traditional intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) to assess the repeatability of speech embeddings learnt by neural network. The proposed ICC regularizer has characteristics similar to well-known contrastive loss which aims to minimize the intra-class variance and maximize the inter-class variance of training data. The authors propose to use the ICC regularizer not in isolation but in combination with the contrastive loss. This paper performs several types of experiments based on speech processing tasks such as speaker verification, voice style conversion, and dysphonic voice detection to demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed regularizer. These tasks focus on the importance of detecting the repeatability for the target data.

Strengths:
- The considered scenario is interesting and important to the community. The proposed method is simple yet effective in assessing the repeatability in the target data.
- This paper assesses the effectiveness of the proposed method not only with a single speech task but also with several tasks based on voice quality evaluation. The improvements look sufficient.

Weaknesses:
- ICC is very traditional metric, which is widely used for various data classification algorithms.
- The proposed ICC regularizer shouldn't be used in isolation.
- Lack of some experiments and details of experimental setup (see questions below).

Questions:
- It looks like that the proposed ICC regularizer corresponds to the special case of contrastive loss focused only on positive sample pairs. Although there are many variants in contrastive loss, the basic concept is to increase a capability of category classification of data by leveraging both positive and negative pairs. I am wondering if the training only with conventional contrastive loss could provide some improvements similar to the proposed method, for example, by simply changing the ratio of positive sample pairs (reducing negative sample pairs) in the last half of the training stage. What are advantages that cannot be obtained by just changing the ratio of positive and negative pair data?
- It is difficult to understand the effectiveness of the proposed method correctly because experimental setups including data are very briefly written. For example, in the experiment described in Section 4.1, what are the training strategy of the model such as an optimizer, a learning rate scheduling, other regularization techniques (specAugment, DropConnect, etc.), data augmentation, and so on?
- In addition, a summary of data used for each experiment (a few more details) would be very much helpful.

Limitations:
The same comments as one of weaknesses. The proposed ICC regularizer shouldn't be used in isolation but be used in combination with a conventional contrastive loss as clearly mentioned in the paper.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper introduces a novel approach to learning robust speech embeddings by leveraging the traditional concept of intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) from measurement theory. The authors propose using ICC not only as an evaluation metric for embedding repeatability but also as a regularizer during neural network training. The core idea is to guide deep neural networks to produce embeddings that are highly sensitive to changes in the label of interest (e.g., speaker identity, clinical class) while remaining invariant to other confounding factors, a property the authors term "repeatability." The proposed ICC regularizer, when used in conjunction with conventional contrastive losses, aims to explicitly minimize intra-class variance and maximize inter-class variance in the learned representations. The manuscript demonstrates the effectiveness of this approach across three distinct speech processing tasks: text-independent speaker verification, zero-shot voice style conversion, and a clinical application for detecting dysphonic voice. These tasks inherently benefit from embeddings that exhibit high repeatability, as consistency within a class is crucial for their performance.

Strengths:
The considered scenario—explicitly incorporating repeatability into embedding learning—is highly interesting and important to the machine learning and speech processing communities. The paper effectively argues that directly assessing and optimizing for repeatability, rather than relying solely on indirect evaluation through downstream task performance, can significantly improve the quality of learned latent representations. The proposed method is conceptually simple, building upon the well-understood ICC 1-1 formulation (Equation 2) to quantify and regularize this property. Despite its simplicity, the method proves to be effective in enhancing the desired embedding characteristics.

Furthermore, this paper assesses the effectiveness of the proposed method not only with a single speech task but also with several diverse tasks based on voice quality evaluation, demonstrating its broad applicability and robustness. The experimental results, detailed in Section 4, show consistent and sufficient improvements across all three applications. For instance, in the text-independent speaker verification (TI-SV) task, the ICC regularizer reduces the Equal Error Rate (EER) by approximately 10% for the VGG-M-40 model when combined with GE2E loss (from 4.39% to 3.96%, Table 1), alongside a notable increase in the ICC score from 0.4494 to 0.5487. In zero-shot voice style conversion, embeddings trained with the ICC regularizer were preferred in 61.9% of AB preference tests (Table 2), and objective metrics also showed improvements (Table 3). For the clinical application of dysphonic voice detection, the proposed method achieved a 30.68% improvement in the ICC score (from 0.4368 to 0.5708) compared to a baseline, leading to better classification accuracy and generalizability across different corpora (Table 4). These comprehensive evaluations underscore the practical utility of the ICC regularizer.

Weaknesses:
ICC is a very traditional metric, which is widely used for various data classification algorithms in statistics and measurement theory. While its application to deep learning embeddings for explicit regularization is novel, the metric itself is well-established, which might lead some to perceive it as less groundbreaking from a purely methodological perspective, despite its effective adaptation.

The proposed ICC regularizer shouldn't be used in isolation, as the authors clearly state in Section 3.2. This is a crucial limitation because the ICC is calculated independently for each embedding dimension (Equation 5). Relying solely on per-dimension repeatability does not guarantee good separability in the overall high-dimensional embedding space and could potentially lead to highly correlated embedding dimensions, which can negatively impact downstream task performance. Therefore, its complementary role with contrastive losses is essential for optimal performance.

There is a lack of some experiments and details of experimental setup, which makes it challenging to fully reproduce or critically evaluate the proposed method's performance. (See questions below for specific examples.)

Questions:
It looks like that the proposed ICC regularizer corresponds to the special case of contrastive loss focused only on positive sample pairs, given its emphasis on minimizing intra-class variance (M SW). Although there are many variants in contrastive loss, the basic concept is to increase a capability of category classification of data by leveraging both positive and negative pairs. I am wondering if the training only with conventional contrastive loss could provide some improvements similar to the proposed method, for example, by simply changing the ratio of positive sample pairs (reducing negative sample pairs) in the last half of the training stage. What are advantages that cannot be obtained by just changing the ratio of positive and negative pair data? The manuscript explains in Section 3.2 and Figure 1 that the ICC regularizer's optimization landscape is distinct from GE2E loss; it primarily focuses on decreasing intra-class variance, especially when inter-class variance is already sufficient. This behavior, driven by its gradient formulation (Equations 7 and 8), allows it to push embeddings towards lower intra-class variance without unnecessarily increasing inter-class variance, which might be a unique advantage not easily replicated by simply tuning contrastive loss parameters.

It is difficult to understand the effectiveness of the proposed method correctly because experimental setups including data are very briefly written for certain aspects. For example, in the experiment described in Section 4.1 (Text-Independent Speaker Verification), while some details are provided (Adam optimizer, static learning rate of 0.001, dropout rate of 0.2, and specific data augmentations like varying audio length and adding Gaussian noise), the description does not explicitly mention other common regularization techniques (e.g., SpecAugment, DropConnect) or learning rate scheduling strategies that might have been considered or used. Clarifying whether these were intentionally omitted or simply not applied would be helpful.

In addition, a summary of data used for each experiment with a few more details would be very much helpful. While Appendix D provides an overview of the datasets (VoxCeleb 1 & 2, VCTK, MEEI, SVD, HUPA, ALS), including more specific statistics (e.g., number of speakers, total duration, type of recordings) relevant to each task in the main experimental sections or a dedicated table would further enhance clarity and reproducibility.

Limitations:
The same comments as one of weaknesses. The proposed ICC regularizer shouldn't be used in isolation but be used in combination with a conventional contrastive loss, as clearly mentioned in the paper (Section 3.2). This is due to its per-dimension calculation, which, if used alone, might lead to suboptimal overall embedding space separability and correlated dimensions. The necessity of hyperparameter fine-tuning for the weighting coefficient λ in the combined loss (Lcontr + λRICC) also adds a practical consideration for deployment.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

title: PromptRestorer: A Prompting Image Restoration Method with Degradation Perception

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a prompting image restoration method with degradation perception. The authors show that raw degradation features can effectively guide deep restoration models, providing accurate degradation priors to facilitate better restoration. To perceive the degradation, the authors propose prompting degradation perception modulator. To control the propagation of the perceived content for restoration branch, the authors propose gated degradation perception propagation.
reviews.strengths:
+ This paper is professionally wrote and presents a better organization.
+ The proposed prompting image restoration method is interesting, which clearly demonstrate that the raw degradation features are better guidance than the learnable parameters used in previous methods.
+ The proposed prompting degradation perception modulator is novel. It has potential applications to latter features fusion manner.
+ The gated degradation perception propagation seems to be useful.
reviews.weaknesses:
1, The author claim that the pre-trained model is the same with the learnable conditional network. Hence, the FLOPs in Tab. 5 should be same.

2, The authors would better to explain why the raw degradation features are better than features from learnable parameters network since more learnable parameters should have better results within the similar network framework.
3, SFT is a commonly used feature fusion module, is the proposed PromptDPM than that? What is the result if replacing the PromptDPM with SFT?
reviews.questions: see weakness
reviews.limitations: see weakness
