reviews.summary:
- The paper explores the use of RTRL in the case of scenario, where no approximation is needed.
- The proposed "method" is not novel per se i.e., Mozer et. al put forward the derivation of RTRL for element-wise recurrence, but to the best of reviewer's ability no one has formally wrote about the tractability of the RTRL in case of some of the RNN architectures (Quasi-RNN or Simple Recurrent Units).
- The paper does experiments for RL, where on different experiments the paper shows the method trained with RTRL can match or even sometimes outperform Truncated Backprop through time. 

==== 

Read the author's rebuttal.
reviews.strengths:
- The paper is very well written.
- The idea of using RTRL for RNNs in which element-wise recurrent is present is interesting.
- The paper does experiments on many RL tasks, and compares the proposed method to TBPTT.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Since the paper is about how well RTRL works with some RNNs, it will be useful to evaluate the proposed method on more tasks like language modelling or sequential image classification etc. 
- For all the experiments, it will also be useful to report a BPTT baseline i.e., where we backprop through the entire "episode". 
- It will also be useful to compare to various other approximations of RTRL (using the vanilla LSTM/GRU) or even consider the tasks consider in SnAp-1. 
- Not a weakness per se, but this is incorrect "Silver et al. [12] explore random projections of the sensitivity". DODGE work explores not just the random projections, but also learning the candidate direction (as a result of auxiliary tasks or via RL like synthetic gradients).
reviews.questions:
- Evaluate the proposed method on more tasks like language modelling, sequential image classification as well as compare to SnAp-1.
- Report the BPTT baseline, as well as some approximation of RTRL to just see how well those approximations work for the problems chosen in this work. 
- It will be helpful to make sure similar setup is followed throughout the experiments. For example: for some experiments, the paper uses the pre-trained visual encoder, or trains the visual encoder via the truncated BPTT gradient.
reviews.limitations: See Weaknesses and Questions.
reviews.rating: 4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
- The paper explores the use of RTRL in the case of scenario, where no approximation is needed.
- The proposed "method" is not novel per se i.e., Mozer et. al put forward the derivation of RTRL for element-wise recurrence, but to the best of reviewer's ability no one has formally wrote about the tractability of the RTRL in case of some of the RNN architectures (Quasi-RNN or Simple Recurrent Units).
- The paper does experiments for RL, where on different experiments the paper shows the method trained with RTRL can match or even sometimes outperform Truncated Backprop through time. 

==== 

Read the author's rebuttal.

Strengths:
- The paper is very well written.
- The idea of using RTRL for RNNs in which element-wise recurrent is present is interesting.
- The paper does experiments on many RL tasks, and compares the proposed method to TBPTT.

Weaknesses:
- Since the paper is about how well RTRL works with some RNNs, it will be useful to evaluate the proposed method on more tasks like language modelling or sequential image classification etc. 
- For all the experiments, it will also be useful to report a BPTT baseline i.e., where we backprop through the entire "episode". 
- It will also be useful to compare to various other approximations of RTRL (using the vanilla LSTM/GRU) or even consider the tasks consider in SnAp-1. 
- Not a weakness per se, but this is incorrect "Silver et al. [12] explore random projections of the sensitivity". DODGE work explores not just the random projections, but also learning the candidate direction (as a result of auxiliary tasks or via RL like synthetic gradients).

Questions:
- Evaluate the proposed method on more tasks like language modelling, sequential image classification as well as compare to SnAp-1.
- Report the BPTT baseline, as well as some approximation of RTRL to just see how well those approximations work for the problems chosen in this work. 
- It will be helpful to make sure similar setup is followed throughout the experiments. For example: for some experiments, the paper uses the pre-trained visual encoder, or trains the visual encoder via the truncated BPTT gradient.

Limitations:
See Weaknesses and Questions.

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

Summary:
The paper delves into the practical application of Real-Time Recurrent Learning (RTRL) for recurrent neural networks (RNNs), specifically focusing on scenarios where its high computational complexity can be mitigated without resorting to approximations. The core of the proposed "method" is not entirely novel in its underlying principle, as previous work by Mozer et al. (lines 62-63, 170-171) already explored the derivation of RTRL for RNN architectures exhibiting element-wise recurrence. However, the manuscript makes a valuable contribution by formally discussing and highlighting the tractability of exact RTRL for several modern RNN architectures, such as Quasi-RNNs (lines 58, 131), Simple Recurrent Units (lines 58, 131), and even certain Linear Transformers (lines 59, 172-174), which is a point not widely emphasized in prior literature. The authors then apply this tractable RTRL variant within an actor-critic framework for reinforcement learning (RL). The experimental section demonstrates that this RTRL-based method can achieve competitive or even superior performance compared to Truncated Backpropagation Through Time (TBPTT) on various RL tasks, particularly those requiring long-span memory.

Read the author's rebuttal.

Strengths:
- The paper is exceptionally well written, demonstrating clarity and precision in its explanations of complex algorithms like RTRL and BPTT (Sec. 2). The structure is logical, guiding the reader through the theoretical underpinnings, the proposed eLSTM architecture (Sec. 3.1), and the experimental setup with ease. The derivations for RTRL for element-wise recurrence, particularly the compact summary in Eqs. 11-12, are clear and well-presented.
- The idea of leveraging RTRL for RNNs characterized by element-wise recurrence is genuinely interesting and addresses a fundamental challenge in the field (lines 55-56). By identifying architectural properties that allow for tractable RTRL without approximation, the paper opens up new avenues for exploring the full potential of RTRL, free from the confound of approximation quality (lines 175-177). The reduction in space and time complexity to O(N^2) (lines 164-165) for the sensitivity matrix computation in the eLSTM is a significant practical advantage over the prohibitive O(N^3) and O(N^4) complexities of fully recurrent NNs (lines 28-29, 116-118).
- The paper features a comprehensive experimental evaluation across a diverse set of reinforcement learning tasks (Sec. 4). This includes challenging DMLab-30 memory tasks (rooms_select_nonmatching_object and rooms_watermaze), ProcGen (Chaser), and five Atari-2600 environments (Breakout, Gravitar, MsPacman, Q*Bert, Seaquest). The comparison against TBPTT is thorough, especially on DMLab memory tasks where the RTRL system is shown to be competitive with or outperform strong baselines like IMPALA and R2D2, often with fewer environmental frames (Table 1, lines 240-242). The authors also carefully control for variables, ensuring that the only difference between the RTRL and TBPTT systems is the gradient computation method (truncated vs. untruncated), allowing for a fair comparison (lines 197-199).

Weaknesses:
- The evaluation, while extensive in the RL domain, would benefit from being expanded to a broader range of sequence-processing tasks. Evaluating the proposed method on tasks like language modeling, sequential image classification, or other time-series prediction problems could reveal different aspects of RTRL's performance and generalizability beyond the specific challenges of RL. These tasks often have different sequence length characteristics and error signal frequencies, which might highlight unique strengths or weaknesses of RTRL that are not apparent in the current RL setup.
- For all experiments, it would be highly beneficial to report a full BPTT baseline, where gradients are backpropagated through the entire episode or sequence without any truncation. While the paper adequately compares against TBPTT, a full BPTT baseline would serve as an important theoretical upper bound for gradient accuracy, providing a more complete picture of the benefits of untruncated gradients versus *any* form of truncation. Although full BPTT might be computationally intractable for very long sequences, even a comparison on shorter tasks or with smaller models could offer valuable insights.
- The paper explicitly aims to explore RTRL without approximation. However, to fully contextualize the "practical promise" of this exact RTRL, it would be useful to compare its performance against various *approximate* RTRL methods (e.g., those using vanilla LSTM/GRU architectures) on their respective benchmarks. Specifically, considering tasks explored by methods like SnAp-1 (Menick et al. [11], mentioned in line 175) would help the reader understand where this exact, tractable RTRL stands in relation to the state-of-the-art in approximate RTRL, which is the dominant direction in current RTRL research.
- The statement "Silver et al. [12] explore random projections of the sensitivity" (line 51) needs correction. The DODGE work by Silver et al. [12] explores not just random projections for sensitivity matrix approximation, but also novel approaches for learning candidate directions, for instance, through auxiliary tasks or using RL-like synthetic gradients. This broader scope should be accurately reflected.

Questions:
- As mentioned in the weaknesses, it would be valuable to evaluate the proposed method on a wider array of tasks, such as language modeling or sequential image classification. How does the eLSTM with tractable RTRL perform on these different types of sequence processing problems, especially compared to standard LSTMs trained with TBPTT?
- Could the authors report a full BPTT baseline for at least a subset of the experiments, particularly those with shorter episode lengths or where computational resources allow? This would provide a more complete comparison regarding the impact of untruncated gradients.
- To what extent do the benefits of exact RTRL, as demonstrated here, outweigh the potential advantages of approximate RTRL methods that can be applied to more general RNN architectures (like LSTMs/GRUs)? A comparison with methods like SnAp-1 on relevant tasks would help to clarify this trade-off.
- The experimental setup sometimes involves pre-trained visual encoders or trains the visual encoder using truncated BPTT gradients (Sec. 4.2, lines 231-233). This is a pragmatic choice, as acknowledged in the limitations (Sec. 5, lines 300-302), but it means not all parameters in the end-to-end system are trained with RTRL. Could the authors elaborate on the implications of this hybrid training approach? How much of the observed performance gain is attributable solely to the recurrent layer being trained with RTRL, and how might a truly end-to-end RTRL-trained system perform if the vision stem could also be trained with untruncated gradients (even if approximated)?

Limitations:
The limitations section in the manuscript (Sec. 5) is well-articulated, and many of the points raised in the weaknesses and questions above further underscore these inherent challenges. The primary limitation highlighted by the authors, the intractability of multi-layer RTRL even for element-wise recurrent networks (lines 280-295), is crucial. This means the current method is restricted to a single recurrent layer, which is a significant constraint in the context of modern deep learning architectures that rely heavily on stacking multiple layers. Furthermore, the necessity of training upstream feedforward components (like the vision stem in RL tasks) with TBPTT (lines 300-302) means that the full end-to-end learning pipeline is not truly RTRL-driven, limiting the scope of its practical application. The discussion also correctly points out that for many tasks, TBPTT with a sufficiently large span might suffice (lines 310-313), questioning the *absolute* necessity of RTRL, and that online learning benefits of RTRL are still an open engineering challenge for sequence-level parallelism (lines 317-323). These points, combined with the limited evaluation scope outside of RL and the lack of comparison to approximate RTRL methods, suggest that while the paper makes a technically solid step, its practical implications and broader utility remain somewhat constrained by these factors.

Rating: 4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
- The paper explores the use of RTRL in the case of scenario, where no approximation is needed.
- The proposed "method" is not novel per se i.e., Mozer et. al put forward the derivation of RTRL for element-wise recurrence, but to the best of reviewer's ability no one has formally wrote about the tractability of the RTRL in case of some of the RNN architectures (Quasi-RNN or Simple Recurrent Units).
- The paper does experiments for RL, where on different experiments the paper shows the method trained with RTRL can match or even sometimes outperform Truncated Backprop through time. 

==== 

Read the author's rebuttal.

Strengths:
- The paper is very well written.
- The idea of using RTRL for RNNs in which element-wise recurrent is present is interesting.
- The paper does experiments on many RL tasks, and compares the proposed method to TBPTT.

Weaknesses:
- Since the paper is about how well RTRL works with some RNNs, it will be useful to evaluate the proposed method on more tasks like language modelling or sequential image classification etc. 
- For all the experiments, it will also be useful to report a BPTT baseline i.e., where we backprop through the entire "episode". 
- It will also be useful to compare to various other approximations of RTRL (using the vanilla LSTM/GRU) or even consider the tasks consider in SnAp-1. 
- Not a weakness per se, but this is incorrect "Silver et al. [12] explore random projections of the sensitivity". DODGE work explores not just the random projections, but also learning the candidate direction (as a result of auxiliary tasks or via RL like synthetic gradients).

Questions:
- Evaluate the proposed method on more tasks like language modelling, sequential image classification as well as compare to SnAp-1.
- Report the BPTT baseline, as well as some approximation of RTRL to just see how well those approximations work for the problems chosen in this work. 
- It will be helpful to make sure similar setup is followed throughout the experiments. For example: for some experiments, the paper uses the pre-trained visual encoder, or trains the visual encoder via the truncated BPTT gradient.

Limitations:
See Weaknesses and Questions.

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.summary: The paper modifies the LSTM architecture so that RTRL complexity, which is usually O(n^4), becomes O(n^2) where n is the number of recurrent units. This is achieved by constraining the recurrent weights to a diagonal structure. After performing some diagnostic tests, the paper presents experimental results using the proposed eLSTM in actor-critic RL settings, specifically DMLab, ProcGen, and Atari. The paper concludes with a discussion on the complexity of RTRL in the multi-layer case.
reviews.strengths:
The paper is well written and clearly structured. 

While the ideas presented in the paper are not novel per se, the application of these ideas to a modern RNN architecture in the context of RL is quite novel. 

The work is quite relevant for the RNN/RL communities as unlocking RTRL for large-scale recurrent models is a long-standing problem and RL is an important field of application for RNNs. 

The paper discusses some rarely addressed limitations about the complexity of RTRL in the multi-layer case as well as some other interesting points.
reviews.weaknesses:
The main weakness of the paper is the empirical evaluation of the method. This ranges from an incrompehensible selection of tasks over a severe lack of external baselines to insufficient evaluation methods (e.g., lack of significance testing). In the following, I will elaborate in greater detail on these and other shortcomings. 

On Procgen and Atari, the paper lacks comparison to state-of-the-art methods or any other external baselines. In general, the authors seem to confuse the concepts of ablation studies and external baselines. For instance, TBPTT on the eLSTM architecture is an ablation study while TBPTT on the fully recurrent LSTM architecture is a baseline (which is missing in all experiments but should be included; see, e.g., [3] for design choices). The authors should compare to state-of-the-art baselines in all environments, not just DMLab-30. 

The paper should experimentally explore the limitations arising from the diagonal constraint on the hidden-to-hidden interactions. This limitation competes with the bias in the gradient estimate used by the companion learning algorithm and SnAp-1 for LSTM. The authors should compare these RTRL variants in experiments to justify their proposed method as opposed to existing ones. 

The core mechanism described in Eq. (5) that reduces the RTRL complexity has been proposed in [1] and should be cited properly. 

In all RL experiments, the authors report mean and standard deviation over only 3 seeds (at least for DMLab-30 and Procgen; for Atari I didn't find any info) without any significance testing. In particular, the use of the standard deviation as a measure of uncertainty in combination with a low number of seeds has been critisized as bad practice and should be replaced by interval estimates such as IQM [2]. 

The paper states that it focuses on "RTRL-based algorithms beyond diagnostic tasks." However, it limits these experiments to the realm of RL, where the importance of memory is often unclear. The authors should investigate how the architectural changes affect performance in some standard supervised tasks. 

[1] Felix A. Gers, Jürgen Schmidhuber:
Recurrent Nets that Time and Count. IJCNN (3) 2000: 189-194

[2] Rishabh Agarwal, Max Schwarzer, Pablo Samuel Castro, Aaron C. Courville, Marc G. Bellemare:
Deep Reinforcement Learning at the Edge of the Statistical Precipice. NeurIPS 2021: 29304-29320

[3] Tianwei Ni, Benjamin Eysenbach, Ruslan Salakhutdinov:
Recurrent Model-Free RL Can Be a Strong Baseline for Many POMDPs. ICML 2022: 16691-16723
reviews.questions:
The proposed eLSTM architecture does not use any cell state activation function. This does not affect the complexity of RTRL so I wonder why this slightly off-standard decision was made. 

Line 21, 53, and 290: shouldn't the word "quadratic" be replaced by "quartic"? 

Why did the authors dedicate resources to reactive tasks where no benefit are to be expected or an RNN might not be helpful to begin with? I had the impression that resources for experiments are limited. This exacerbates the question why reactive tasks were explored. 

The paper states its focus is "to evaluate learning with untruncated gradients rather than the potential for online learning." I wonder on what basis this decision was made as online updates could lead to high sample efficiency, one of the key problems in RL. I feel the paper missed a big chance by not researching this connection of online updates and sample efficiency. Instead, it focussed on reactive tasks. 

The paper states that "both the feedforward and LSTM baselines perform similarly" in Procgen Memory mode. However, [5] shows that the feedforward baseline is outperformed by methods using memory in 4/6 environments. Moreover, the Procgen paper [4] (see appendix H) shows that LSTM or framestacking outperforms feedforward in 4/6 envirnments even in non-memory mode. 

In summary, I really like the presented method (and the way it was presented) and believe that it bears quite some potential. However, the empirical evaluation is very weak and I do not believe it can be fixed in the short rebuttal period. Therefore, publication of the paper seems premature at this point. 

[4] Karl Cobbe, Christopher Hesse, Jacob Hilton, John Schulman:
Leveraging Procedural Generation to Benchmark Reinforcement Learning. ICML 2020: 2048-2056

[5] Fabian Paischer, Thomas Adler, Vihang P. Patil, Angela Bitto-Nemling, Markus Holzleitner, Sebastian Lehner, Hamid Eghbal-Zadeh, Sepp Hochreiter:
History Compression via Language Models in Reinforcement Learning. ICML 2022: 17156-17185
reviews.limitations: The authors give some interesting discussion on the limitation of their work, in particular the multi-layer RNN case.
reviews.rating: 4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper modifies the LSTM architecture so that RTRL complexity, which is usually O(n^4), becomes O(n^2) where n is the number of recurrent units. This is achieved by constraining the recurrent weights to a diagonal structure. After performing some diagnostic tests, the paper presents experimental results using the proposed eLSTM in actor-critic RL settings, specifically DMLab, ProcGen, and Atari. The paper concludes with a discussion on the complexity of RTRL in the multi-layer case.

Strengths:
The paper is well written and clearly structured. 

While the ideas presented in the paper are not novel per se, the application of these ideas to a modern RNN architecture in the context of RL is quite novel. 

The work is quite relevant for the RNN/RL communities as unlocking RTRL for large-scale recurrent models is a long-standing problem and RL is an important field of application for RNNs. 

The paper discusses some rarely addressed limitations about the complexity of RTRL in the multi-layer case as well as some other interesting points.

Weaknesses:
The main weakness of the paper is the empirical evaluation of the method. This ranges from an incrompehensible selection of tasks over a severe lack of external baselines to insufficient evaluation methods (e.g., lack of significance testing). In the following, I will elaborate in greater detail on these and other shortcomings. 

On Procgen and Atari, the paper lacks comparison to state-of-the-art methods or any other external baselines. In general, the authors seem to confuse the concepts of ablation studies and external baselines. For instance, TBPTT on the eLSTM architecture is an ablation study while TBPTT on the fully recurrent LSTM architecture is a baseline (which is missing in all experiments but should be included; see, e.g., [3] for design choices). The authors should compare to state-of-the-art baselines in all environments, not just DMLab-30. 

The paper should experimentally explore the limitations arising from the diagonal constraint on the hidden-to-hidden interactions. This limitation competes with the bias in the gradient estimate used by the companion learning algorithm and SnAp-1 for LSTM. The authors should compare these RTRL variants in experiments to justify their proposed method as opposed to existing ones. 

The core mechanism described in Eq. (5) that reduces the RTRL complexity has been proposed in [1] and should be cited properly. 

In all RL experiments, the authors report mean and standard deviation over only 3 seeds (at least for DMLab-30 and Procgen; for Atari I didn't find any info) without any significance testing. In particular, the use of the standard deviation as a measure of uncertainty in combination with a low number of seeds has been critisized as bad practice and should be replaced by interval estimates such as IQM [2]. 

The paper states that it focuses on "RTRL-based algorithms beyond diagnostic tasks." However, it limits these experiments to the realm of RL, where the importance of memory is often unclear. The authors should investigate how the architectural changes affect performance in some standard supervised tasks. 

[1] Felix A. Gers, Jürgen Schmidhuber:
Recurrent Nets that Time and Count. IJCNN (3) 2000: 189-194

[2] Rishabh Agarwal, Max Schwarzer, Pablo Samuel Castro, Aaron C. Courville, Marc G. Bellemare:
Deep Reinforcement Learning at the Edge of the Statistical Precipice. NeurIPS 2021: 29304-29320

[3] Tianwei Ni, Benjamin Eysenbach, Ruslan Salakhutdinov:
Recurrent Model-Free RL Can Be a Strong Baseline for Many POMDPs. ICML 2022: 16691-16723

Questions:
The proposed eLSTM architecture does not use any cell state activation function. This does not affect the complexity of RTRL so I wonder why this slightly off-standard decision was made. 

Line 21, 53, and 290: shouldn't the word "quadratic" be replaced by "quartic"? 

Why did the authors dedicate resources to reactive tasks where no benefit are to be expected or an RNN might not be helpful to begin with? I had the impression that resources for experiments are limited. This exacerbates the question why reactive tasks were explored. 

The paper states its focus is "to evaluate learning with untruncated gradients rather than the potential for online learning." I wonder on what basis this decision was made as online updates could lead to high sample efficiency, one of the key problems in RL. I feel the paper missed a big chance by not researching this connection of online updates and sample efficiency. Instead, it focussed on reactive tasks. 

The paper states that "both the feedforward and LSTM baselines perform similarly" in Procgen Memory mode. However, [5] shows that the feedforward baseline is outperformed by methods using memory in 4/6 environments. Moreover, the Procgen paper [4] (see appendix H) shows that LSTM or framestacking outperforms feedforward in 4/6 envirnments even in non-memory mode. 

In summary, I really like the presented method (and the way it was presented) and believe that it bears quite some potential. However, the empirical evaluation is very weak and I do not believe it can be fixed in the short rebuttal period. Therefore, publication of the paper seems premature at this point. 

[4] Karl Cobbe, Christopher Hesse, Jacob Hilton, John Schulman:
Leveraging Procedural Generation to Benchmark Reinforcement Learning. ICML 2020: 2048-2056

[5] Fabian Paischer, Thomas Adler, Vihang P. Patil, Angela Bitto-Nemling, Markus Holzleitner, Sebastian Lehner, Hamid Eghbal-Zadeh, Sepp Hochreiter:
History Compression via Language Models in Reinforcement Learning. ICML 2022: 17156-17185

Limitations:
The authors give some interesting discussion on the limitation of their work, in particular the multi-layer RNN case.

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper presents an intriguing modification to the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) architecture, termed eLSTM, which aims to address the notorious computational complexity of Real-Time Recurrent Learning (RTRL). By constraining the recurrent weights to an element-wise or diagonal structure, the authors successfully reduce the per-step time and space complexity of exact RTRL from the typical O(N^4) and O(N^3) for a fully recurrent network, respectively, to a more tractable O(N^2) for both. This architectural innovation, detailed in Section 3.1 and specifically equations 8-12, enables the application of exact RTRL without approximations, moving beyond the diagnostic settings often seen in prior RTRL research. After validating the eLSTM in a classic diagnostic task (copy task, Section 4.1), the paper primarily focuses on presenting experimental results of this proposed eLSTM integrated into an actor-critic reinforcement learning (RL) framework, named R2AC, across various challenging domains including DMLab, ProcGen, and Atari. The work concludes with a thoughtful and critical discussion in Section 5, highlighting inherent limitations of RTRL, particularly concerning its complexity in multi-layer deep learning architectures.

Strengths:
The paper is well written and clearly structured, providing a logical flow from background to method, experiments, and discussion of limitations. The explanations of RTRL and the eLSTM architecture, particularly in Section 2 and 3.1, are easy to follow and the derivations in Appendix A.1 are comprehensive. This clarity greatly aids in understanding the technical contributions.

While the ideas presented in the paper, such as the use of element-wise recurrence to simplify gradient computation, are not novel per se (as acknowledged by the authors themselves, citing Mozer [18, 19] from the late 1980s and Javed et al. [20, 21]), the application of these specific ideas to a modern RNN architecture like LSTM within the context of deep reinforcement learning is quite novel. The explicit demonstration of tractable RTRL for eLSTM (Section 3.1) and its testing in complex environments like DMLab-30 and ProcGen represents a significant step towards bridging the gap between theoretical RTRL advantages and practical deep learning applications.

The work is quite relevant for the RNN/RL communities as unlocking RTRL for large-scale recurrent models is a long-standing problem. The conceptual advantages of RTRL, such as untruncated gradients and online learning, are highly attractive for long-horizon credit assignment problems in RL, making this field an important application area for RNNs. The paper's exploration of these advantages in realistic settings, particularly on memory-intensive DMLab tasks like rooms_watermaze where RTRL shows competitive or superior performance to baselines (Table 1, Figure 1b, 1e), directly addresses a key challenge in the field.

The paper discusses some rarely addressed limitations about the complexity of RTRL in the multi-layer case as well as some other interesting points. Section 5, "Limitations and Discussion," is particularly commendable for its candid and detailed analysis of these challenges, including the intractability of stacking eLSTM layers (equations 13-16), the complexity introduced by feedforward layers preceding the RNN, and the quadratic growth of sensitivity matrices with layer depth. These insights provide valuable directions for future research on practical RTRL.

Weaknesses:
The main weakness of the paper is the empirical evaluation of the method. This ranges from an incomprehensible selection of tasks over a severe lack of external baselines to insufficient evaluation methods (e.g., lack of significance testing). The choice to dedicate significant experimental resources to tasks where the benefits of recurrence or untruncated gradients are not expected, such as the DMLab reactive task rooms_keys_doors_puzzle (Section 4.3, Table 2) and certain Atari environments, seems suboptimal given the stated focus on "RTRL-based algorithms beyond diagnostic tasks" and the clear limitations on experimental compute. In the following, I will elaborate in greater detail on these and other shortcomings.

On Procgen and Atari, the paper lacks comparison to state-of-the-art methods or any other external baselines. For instance, in ProcGen (Section 4.2, Figure 1c, 1f) and Atari (Section 4.3, Figure 2), the only comparison is against TBPTT using the *same* eLSTM architecture and a feedforward baseline. In general, the authors seem to confuse the concepts of ablation studies and external baselines. While comparing RTRL to TBPTT on the eLSTM architecture (e.g., in Table 1) is a valuable ablation study to isolate the effect of untruncated gradients, it does not replace a comparison against established methods. For instance, TBPTT on a standard, fully recurrent LSTM architecture (e.g., the one used in IMPALA [31] or R2D2 [32]), which does not have the diagonal constraint, is a crucial baseline that is missing in all experiments but should be included. This would directly show whether the architectural simplification for RTRL (the eLSTM) compromises performance compared to a standard, more expressive LSTM trained with TBPTT, a key consideration for its practical viability. The authors should compare to state-of-the-art baselines in all environments, not just DMLab-30. The DMLab-30 comparison in Table 1 against IMPALA and R2D2 from [32] is a good start, but similar comparisons are needed for ProcGen and Atari to demonstrate the general competitiveness of R2AC.

The paper should experimentally explore the limitations arising from the diagonal constraint on the hidden-to-hidden interactions. While the eLSTM architecture offers tractability, this simplification might inherently limit the model's capacity for complex temporal dependencies compared to a fully recurrent LSTM. This limitation competes with the bias in the gradient estimate used by the companion learning algorithm (e.g., Hochreiter & Schmidhuber [36]) and SnAp-1 of Menick et al. [11], which are diagonal approximations of RTRL for *fully recurrent* LSTMs. The authors should compare these RTRL variants in experiments to justify their proposed method as opposed to existing ones. Specifically, a comparison against an approximate RTRL method applied to a standard LSTM would clarify whether exact gradients on a constrained architecture are preferable to approximate gradients on a more expressive architecture.

The core mechanism described in Eq. (5) that reduces the RTRL complexity, specifically the element-wise recurrence in the gates (`wf ⊙ c(t-1)` and `wz ⊙ c(t-1)`), has been proposed in [1] (Gers & Schmidhuber, "Recurrent Nets that Time and Count", IJCNN 2000) for a similar LSTM variant and should be cited properly in Section 3.1, ideally around line 127 where the eLSTM architecture is introduced.

In all RL experiments, the authors report mean and standard deviation over only 3 seeds (at least for DMLab-30 and Procgen, as stated in Table 1 footnote and implied by Figure 1; for Atari in Figure 2, no explicit seed count is given but the small error bars suggest a similar low number) without any significance testing. In particular, the use of the standard deviation as a measure of uncertainty in combination with a low number of seeds has been criticized as bad practice and should be replaced by interval estimates such as Interquartile Mean (IQM) as recommended by Agarwal et al. [2]. Given the stochasticity inherent in deep RL, small differences in mean performance with high standard deviations (e.g., RTRL vs TBPTT on Gravitar in Table 2, 1670 ± 358 vs 1269 ± 11) make it difficult to draw robust conclusions about the relative performance, especially with only 3 seeds.

The paper states that it focuses on "RTRL-based algorithms beyond diagnostic tasks." However, it limits these experiments to the realm of RL, where the importance of memory is often unclear or task-dependent. While DMLab rooms_watermaze (Table 1) clearly benefits from long-span credit assignments, other tasks like ProcGen Chaser (Figure 1c, 1f) and several Atari games show less pronounced or no benefits. The authors should investigate how the architectural changes affect performance in some standard supervised sequence-processing tasks, such as language modeling, time series prediction, or sequence classification, where the benefits of untruncated gradients might be more directly observable and interpretable without the added complexities of the RL loop.

Questions:
The proposed eLSTM architecture (Eq. 5-7 in Section 3.1) does not explicitly use any cell state activation function (e.g., `tanh` on `c(t)` before computing `h(t)`). Standard LSTM formulations typically apply a non-linearity to the cell state before it is passed through the output gate to become the hidden state. This does not affect the complexity of RTRL, as the RTRL derivations focus on the recurrent state `c(t)`, so I wonder why this slightly off-standard decision was made. A brief justification for this architectural choice would be helpful.

Line 21, 53, and 290 use the word "quadratic" to describe the highest complexity of RTRL. Specifically, line 21 states "the time and space complexities of RTRL for a fully recurrent NN are quadratic and cubic in the number of hidden units, respectively". Line 53 mentions "quadratic/cubic complexities". However, Section 2 states the time complexity is O(N^4) (line 116) and space complexity O(N^3) (line 115). Therefore, shouldn't the word "quadratic" in lines 21 and 53 be replaced by "quartic" (for time complexity) or "cubic" (for space complexity) for consistency and accuracy? Similarly, in line 290, "quadratic/cubic time and space complexity" should be updated.

Why did the authors dedicate resources to reactive tasks, such as DMLab rooms_keys_doors_puzzle (Section 4.3, Table 2) and some Atari environments (Section 4.3, Figure 2), where no benefit from long-span memory or untruncated gradients are to be expected or an RNN might not be helpful to begin with? The results on rooms_keys_doors_puzzle, where feedforward, TBPTT, and RTRL perform nearly the same, confirm this lack of benefit. I had the impression that resources for experiments are limited, as suggested by the pre-training strategy for DMLab tasks to reduce compute (Section 4.2, line 226). This exacerbates the question why reactive tasks were explored, rather than focusing solely on memory-intensive tasks or expanding the scope to other domains where RTRL's advantages might be clearer.

The paper states its focus is "to evaluate learning with untruncated gradients rather than the potential for online learning" (Section 3.2, line 201). I wonder on what basis this decision was made as online updates, a unique advantage of RTRL over BPTT, could lead to high sample efficiency, one of the key problems in RL. By accumulating gradients for M steps (where M > 1 in all experiments), the paper deliberately moves away from fully online updates. I feel the paper missed a big chance by not thoroughly researching this connection of online updates and sample efficiency, which could be a primary driver for RTRL adoption. Instead, it focused on reactive tasks where this advantage is less pertinent.

The paper states that "both the feedforward and LSTM baselines perform similarly" in Procgen Memory mode (Section 4.2, line 248). However, this observation contradicts prior findings. Paischer et al. [5] (see their Table 3) shows that the feedforward baseline is significantly outperformed by methods using memory (e.g., LSTM) in 4 out of 6 ProcGen memory environments (e.g., CoinRun, Climber, Starpilot, BigFish). Moreover, the original ProcGen paper [4] (see appendix H) shows that LSTM or frame-stacking often outperforms feedforward agents in 4 out of 6 environments even in non-memory mode, suggesting that memory can be beneficial even in standard settings. This discrepancy requires further investigation or clarification regarding the specific experimental setup or environments tested.

Limitations:
The authors give some interesting discussion on the limitation of their work, in particular the multi-layer RNN case in Section 5. While this discussion is a strength of the paper's critical analysis, the limitations themselves are significant practical hurdles for the proposed method. The most crucial limitation is the restriction of the tractable-RTRL algorithm for element-wise recurrent nets to the one-layer case. As the authors correctly point out, stacking even two such layers leads to an intractable RTRL algorithm with the same complexity bottleneck as fully recurrent networks (equations 13-16). This is a major concern for modern deep learning where multi-layer architectures are standard for achieving high performance. Furthermore, the discussion highlights that even with a single RNN layer, any preceding feedforward layers (like the convolutional vision module in their RL setup) still require TBPTT for training if their parameters are to be updated, limiting the "full untruncated gradient" promise of RTRL. The observation that RTRL's complexity grows quadratically with the number of layers (L(L+1)/2 sensitivity matrices) compared to BPTT's linear growth (Section 5, lines 305-309) presents a fundamental scalability challenge that is not easily overcome.

Summary of Decision:
In summary, I really like the presented method and the way it was presented, as it tackles a long-standing and important problem in recurrent neural networks. The conceptual elegance of achieving exact RTRL for a modern RNN architecture and demonstrating its potential in some challenging RL tasks is commendable. However, the empirical evaluation is very weak due to issues such as insufficient baselines (especially the missing comparison to a standard fully recurrent LSTM with TBPTT), a lack of rigorous statistical analysis with only 3 seeds, and a suboptimal allocation of experimental resources to reactive tasks. These shortcomings severely hinder the ability to draw robust conclusions about the general practical utility and competitiveness of the proposed R2AC method. I do not believe these fundamental issues in the experimental design and evaluation can be fixed in the short rebuttal period. Therefore, despite the promising technical contribution, publication of the paper seems premature at this point.

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper modifies the LSTM architecture so that RTRL complexity, which is usually O(n^4), becomes O(n^2) where n is the number of recurrent units. This is achieved by constraining the recurrent weights to a diagonal structure. After performing some diagnostic tests, the paper presents experimental results using the proposed eLSTM in actor-critic RL settings, specifically DMLab, ProcGen, and Atari. The paper concludes with a discussion on the complexity of RTRL in the multi-layer case.

Strengths:
The paper is well written and clearly structured. 

While the ideas presented in the paper are not novel per se, the application of these ideas to a modern RNN architecture in the context of RL is quite novel. 

The work is quite relevant for the RNN/RL communities as unlocking RTRL for large-scale recurrent models is a long-standing problem and RL is an important field of application for RNNs. 

The paper discusses some rarely addressed limitations about the complexity of RTRL in the multi-layer case as well as some other interesting points.

Weaknesses:
The main weakness of the paper is the empirical evaluation of the method. This ranges from an incrompehensible selection of tasks over a severe lack of external baselines to insufficient evaluation methods (e.g., lack of significance testing). In the following, I will elaborate in greater detail on these and other shortcomings. 

On Procgen and Atari, the paper lacks comparison to state-of-the-art methods or any other external baselines. In general, the authors seem to confuse the concepts of ablation studies and external baselines. For instance, TBPTT on the eLSTM architecture is an ablation study while TBPTT on the fully recurrent LSTM architecture is a baseline (which is missing in all experiments but should be included; see, e.g., [3] for design choices). The authors should compare to state-of-the-art baselines in all environments, not just DMLab-30. 

The paper should experimentally explore the limitations arising from the diagonal constraint on the hidden-to-hidden interactions. This limitation competes with the bias in the gradient estimate used by the companion learning algorithm and SnAp-1 for LSTM. The authors should compare these RTRL variants in experiments to justify their proposed method as opposed to existing ones. 

The core mechanism described in Eq. (5) that reduces the RTRL complexity has been proposed in [1] and should be cited properly. 

In all RL experiments, the authors report mean and standard deviation over only 3 seeds (at least for DMLab-30 and Procgen; for Atari I didn't find any info) without any significance testing. In particular, the use of the standard deviation as a measure of uncertainty in combination with a low number of seeds has been critisized as bad practice and should be replaced by interval estimates such as IQM [2]. 

The paper states that it focuses on "RTRL-based algorithms beyond diagnostic tasks." However, it limits these experiments to the realm of RL, where the importance of memory is often unclear. The authors should investigate how the architectural changes affect performance in some standard supervised tasks. 

[1] Felix A. Gers, Jürgen Schmidhuber:
Recurrent Nets that Time and Count. IJCNN (3) 2000: 189-194

[2] Rishabh Agarwal, Max Schwarzer, Pablo Samuel Castro, Aaron C. Courville, Marc G. Bellemare:
Deep Reinforcement Learning at the Edge of the Statistical Precipice. NeurIPS 2021: 29304-29320

[3] Tianwei Ni, Benjamin Eysenbach, Ruslan Salakhutdinov:
Recurrent Model-Free RL Can Be a Strong Baseline for Many POMDPs. ICML 2022: 16691-16723

Questions:
The proposed eLSTM architecture does not use any cell state activation function. This does not affect the complexity of RTRL so I wonder why this slightly off-standard decision was made. 

Line 21, 53, and 290: shouldn't the word "quadratic" be replaced by "quartic"? 

Why did the authors dedicate resources to reactive tasks where no benefit are to be expected or an RNN might not be helpful to begin with? I had the impression that resources for experiments are limited. This exacerbates the question why reactive tasks were explored. 

The paper states its focus is "to evaluate learning with untruncated gradients rather than the potential for online learning." I wonder on what basis this decision was made as online updates could lead to high sample efficiency, one of the key problems in RL. I feel the paper missed a big chance by not researching this connection of online updates and sample efficiency. Instead, it focussed on reactive tasks. 

The paper states that "both the feedforward and LSTM baselines perform similarly" in Procgen Memory mode. However, [5] shows that the feedforward baseline is outperformed by methods using memory in 4/6 environments. Moreover, the Procgen paper [4] (see appendix H) shows that LSTM or framestacking outperforms feedforward in 4/6 envirnments even in non-memory mode. 

In summary, I really like the presented method (and the way it was presented) and believe that it bears quite some potential. However, the empirical evaluation is very weak and I do not believe it can be fixed in the short rebuttal period. Therefore, publication of the paper seems premature at this point. 

[4] Karl Cobbe, Christopher Hesse, Jacob Hilton, John Schulman:
Leveraging Procedural Generation to Benchmark Reinforcement Learning. ICML 2020: 2048-2056

[5] Fabian Paischer, Thomas Adler, Vihang P. Patil, Angela Bitto-Nemling, Markus Holzleitner, Sebastian Lehner, Hamid Eghbal-Zadeh, Sepp Hochreiter:
History Compression via Language Models in Reinforcement Learning. ICML 2022: 17156-17185

Limitations:
The authors give some interesting discussion on the limitation of their work, in particular the multi-layer RNN case.

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.

title: Towards a Better Theoretical Understanding of Independent Subnetwork Training

reviews.summary:
The paper provides a theoretical analysis of the convergence properties of Independent Subnetwork Training (IST), for distributed Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) optimization with a quadratic loss function. The analysis considers both the cases of homogeneous and heterogeneous distributed scenarios, without restrictive assumptions on the gradient estimator.

The work characterizes situations where IST converges very efficiently, and cases where it does not converge to the optimal solution but to an irreducible neighbourhood. Experimental results that validate the theory are provided in the Appendix.
reviews.strengths:
The paper provides a solid analytical treatment of the important problem of distributed optimization with reduced communication overhead by means of Independent Subnetwork Training (IST).

Compared to previous work, the analysis of the paper does not rely on the restrictive assumption of a bounded stochastic gradient norm.

The paper is well written – the exposition is clear, and the material is well structured and well presented.
reviews.weaknesses:
The work considers distributed Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) training with a quadratic loss. As mentioned by the authors in Section 3, a simple quadratic loss function has been used in other work to analyze properties of neural networks. While this loss function can still provide interesting theoretical insights, it would be valuable to extend the analysis and the experimental results to more generally used loss functions.

Minor comments:

-- Line 17: "drives from" may be changed to "derives from".

-- Equation after line 217: it seems that the first part of the equation
"$\mathbb{E}[g^k] = \bar{\mathbf{L}}^{-1} \bar{\mathbf{L}} x^k \pm \bar{\mathbf{L}}^{-1} \bar{b} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \tilde{\mathbf{D} b} = $ ..." 
should be rewritten as
"$\mathbb{E}[g^k] = \bar{\mathbf{L}}^{-1} \bar{\mathbf{L}} x^k - \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \tilde{\mathbf{D} b} = $ ...".

-- Equation after line 221: it seems that the first part of the equation
"$\mathbb{E}[x^{k+1}] = x^k - \gamma \mathbb{E}[g^k] = $ ..."
should be
"$\mathbb{E}[x^{k+1}] = \mathbb{E}[x^k] - \gamma \mathbb{E}[g^k] = $ ...".
reviews.questions: It would be valuable if the authors could consider a discussion or possible additional experiments to extend some of the results and insights presented in the paper to the case of distributed optimization with more generally used loss functions.
reviews.limitations: The work relates to distributed training of large-scale models, which generally correspond to significant power consumption and CO2 emissions. However, the IST method studied in the paper aims at allowing distributed training with reduced communication overhead, corresponding to potentially reduced power consumption.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Summary:
The paper provides a theoretical analysis of the convergence properties of Independent Subnetwork Training (IST), for distributed Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) optimization with a quadratic loss function. The analysis considers both the cases of homogeneous and heterogeneous distributed scenarios, without restrictive assumptions on the gradient estimator.

The work characterizes situations where IST converges very efficiently, and cases where it does not converge to the optimal solution but to an irreducible neighbourhood. Experimental results that validate the theory are provided in the Appendix.

Strengths:
The paper provides a solid analytical treatment of the important problem of distributed optimization with reduced communication overhead by means of Independent Subnetwork Training (IST).

Compared to previous work, the analysis of the paper does not rely on the restrictive assumption of a bounded stochastic gradient norm.

The paper is well written – the exposition is clear, and the material is well structured and well presented.

Weaknesses:
The work considers distributed Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) training with a quadratic loss. As mentioned by the authors in Section 3, a simple quadratic loss function has been used in other work to analyze properties of neural networks. While this loss function can still provide interesting theoretical insights, it would be valuable to extend the analysis and the experimental results to more generally used loss functions.

Minor comments:

-- Line 17: "drives from" may be changed to "derives from".

-- Equation after line 217: it seems that the first part of the equation
"$\mathbb{E}[g^k] = \bar{\mathbf{L}}^{-1} \bar{\mathbf{L}} x^k \pm \bar{\mathbf{L}}^{-1} \bar{b} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \tilde{\mathbf{D} b} = $ ..." 
should be rewritten as
"$\mathbb{E}[g^k] = \bar{\mathbf{L}}^{-1} \bar{\mathbf{L}} x^k - \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \tilde{\mathbf{D} b} = $ ...".

"$\mathbb{E}[x^{k+1}] = x^k - \gamma \mathbb{E}[g^k] = $ ..."
should be
"$\mathbb{E}[x^{k+1}] = \mathbb{E}[x^k] - \gamma \mathbb{E}[g^k] = $ ...".

Questions:

Limitations:
The work relates to distributed training of large-scale models, which generally correspond to significant power consumption and CO2 emissions. However, the IST method studied in the paper aims at allowing distributed training with reduced communication overhead, corresponding to potentially reduced power consumption.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

Summary:
The paper provides a theoretical analysis of the convergence properties of Independent Subnetwork Training (IST), for distributed Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) optimization with a quadratic loss function. This analysis is particularly timely given the growing need for efficient distributed training strategies for large-scale models, which often face severe communication and memory constraints (lines 3-7). The authors delve into both homogeneous and heterogeneous distributed scenarios, offering a rigorous examination without relying on typical restrictive assumptions often found in prior works, such as a bounded stochastic gradient norm (lines 125-130). The work meticulously characterizes situations where IST can converge very efficiently, such as in the interpolation case with appropriate preconditioning (Section 3.1.1, 3.1.2), and critically identifies cases where it does not converge to the optimal solution but rather to an irreducible neighbourhood (Section 4, Theorem 2), providing a tight characterization of this bias. Experimental results that validate the proposed theory are provided in the Appendix, offering empirical support for the theoretical findings.

Strengths:
The paper provides a solid analytical treatment of the important problem of distributed optimization with reduced communication overhead by means of Independent Subnetwork Training (IST). This approach is highly relevant for addressing the challenges of scaling modern machine learning models, where both data and model parallelism are crucial (lines 11-15, 36-39). The formalization of IST as an iterative procedure in Algorithm 1, combined with the use of permutation sketches (Definition 2, lines 101-105), offers a clear and tractable framework for analysis. The analysis effectively demonstrates how IST can significantly reduce the load on computing nodes and facilitate faster synchronization, which are key benefits for large-scale distributed systems.

Compared to previous work, the analysis of the paper does not rely on the restrictive assumption of a bounded stochastic gradient norm. This is a significant improvement, as the authors correctly point out that this assumption can be "pathological" and lead to vacuous bounds, especially when dealing with biased gradient estimators or heterogeneous data (lines 125-130, 290-292). By avoiding this assumption, the paper provides a more accurate and robust understanding of IST's behavior, particularly in the presence of heterogeneity (Section 4.2). This rigorous approach allows for a deeper insight into the fundamental difficulties of analyzing biased gradient estimators, which are inherent in methods like IST.

The paper is well written – the exposition is clear, and the material is well structured and well presented. The logical flow from introducing the problem (Section 1) to formalizing the setup (Section 2) and then detailing the results for specific cases (Sections 3 and 4) makes the complex theoretical arguments accessible. The authors clearly delineate the "interpolation case" (Section 3) from the "general case" with irreducible bias (Section 4), helping readers understand the nuances of IST's convergence properties. The use of weighted norms in the analysis (lines 160-162, 247-249) is also a strong point, as it allows for tighter and more accurate characterizations of convergence rates compared to standard Euclidean distances.

Weaknesses:
The work considers distributed Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) training with a quadratic loss. As mentioned by the authors in Section 3 (lines 136-144), a simple quadratic loss function has been used in other work to analyze properties of neural networks, serving as a non-trivial model to understand complex optimization algorithms. While this loss function can still provide interesting theoretical insights into phenomena like irreducible bias and the impact of heterogeneity, it represents a simplification compared to the non-convex and often non-smooth loss functions typically encountered in deep learning. It would be valuable to extend the analysis and the experimental results to more generally used loss functions, as this would further bridge the gap between theoretical understanding and practical applications of IST. The challenges of such an extension would be considerable, but the insights gained would be highly impactful.

Minor comments:
-- Line 17: "drives from" may be changed to "derives from".
-- Equation after line 217: it seems that the first part of the equation
"$\mathbb{E}[g^k] = \bar{\mathbf{L}}^{-1} \bar{\mathbf{L}} x^k \pm \bar{\mathbf{L}}^{-1} \bar{b} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \tilde{\mathbf{D} b} = $ ..."
should be rewritten as
"$\mathbb{E}[g^k] = \bar{\mathbf{L}}^{-1} \bar{\mathbf{L}} x^k - \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \tilde{\mathbf{D} b} = $ ...".
-- Equation after line 221: it seems that the first part of the equation
"$\mathbb{E}[x^{k+1}] = x^k - \gamma \mathbb{E}[g^k] = $ ..."
should be
"$\mathbb{E}[x^{k+1}] = \mathbb{E}[x^k] - \gamma \mathbb{E}[g^k] = $ ...".

Questions:
It would be valuable if the authors could consider a discussion or possible additional experiments to extend some of the results and insights presented in the paper to the case of distributed optimization with more generally used loss functions. Specifically, a discussion on how the insights regarding irreducible bias (Section 4) or the effectiveness of preconditioning (Section 3.1) might translate or need to be adapted for non-convex objectives, particularly those prevalent in deep learning, would significantly enhance the paper's practical relevance. This aligns with their future work suggestions (lines 309-311) and could outline a research agenda for tackling these more complex scenarios.

Limitations:
The work relates to distributed training of large-scale models, which generally correspond to significant power consumption and CO2 emissions. However, the IST method studied in the paper aims at allowing distributed training with reduced communication overhead, corresponding to potentially reduced power consumption. By enabling training with smaller subnetworks and transmitting sparse updates (lines 36-39, 204-205), IST directly contributes to communication efficiency. This reduction in communication, in turn, can lead to lower energy consumption for data transfer and processing, thereby offering a more sustainable approach to scaling machine learning models. This inherent efficiency is a notable positive aspect, especially as the environmental impact of large-scale AI training becomes an increasingly important consideration.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations. The paper makes significant theoretical contributions by rigorously analyzing Independent Subnetwork Training, a highly relevant method for communication-efficient distributed optimization. Its novel approach to handling biased gradient estimators and characterizing irreducible neighborhoods, especially without restrictive assumptions, provides fundamental insights into a complex problem. The clear exposition, well-structured arguments, and careful comparison to previous works solidify its technical merit and high impact within the field of distributed machine learning optimization.

Summary:
The authors provided a theoretical analysis for independent subnet training and provided a convergence analysis in the case where communication compression is present.

The authors discussed the scenario when bias is not present and provided two analyses in the homogeneous and heterogeneous case, respectively before extending their theorems to the case with bias.

Strengths:
1. Sections 3 and 4 are well written and clear, which break the scenarios down in an intuitive way and presented the theorems and outlines clearly.

Weaknesses:
1. Introduction could be more straightforward and can dive straight into the main technical contributions of the work. It was not clear why the problem is well motivated and what are the main technical hurdles until reading section 2 and onwards. A clearer presentation in the intro can make the paper much more readable and well-motivated.

1. The authors mentioned that prior work on the convergence of IST focus "on overparameterized single hidden layer neural networks with ReLU activations". 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**
The authors provided a theoretical analysis for independent subnet training and provided a convergence analysis in the case where communication compression is present. Specifically, they focus on Independent Subnetwork Training (IST) which employs sketching operators (like Permutation sketches, Definition 2) to manage communication costs and memory constraints in distributed training. The paper formalizes the IST paradigm as an iterative procedure (Algorithm 1) and meticulously analyzes its optimization performance. The authors discussed the scenario when bias is not present and provided two analyses in the homogeneous and heterogeneous case, respectively before extending their theorems to the case with bias. This structured approach helps in isolating and understanding the unique effects of combined data and model parallelism, particularly concerning the impact of heterogeneity and the emergence of an irreducible bias.

**Strengths:**
1.  Sections 3 and 4 are well written and clear, which break the scenarios down in an intuitive way and presented the theorems and outlines clearly. The exposition effectively guides the reader through increasing complexity, starting from the "interpolation case" without a linear term (Section 3) and progressing to the "general case" with an irreducible bias (Section 4). Theorem 1, for instance, clearly establishes convergence rates for the bias-free scenario, highlighting how the method can match Gradient Descent rates under specific preconditioning strategies (e.g., using the modified Perm-1 sketch in Section 3.1.2). Similarly, Theorem 2 provides a comprehensive analysis for the general case, detailing the irreducible neighborhood and the trade-offs between convergence speed and neighborhood size (lines 234-240). The use of weighted norms in the analysis (lines 159-160, 246-247) is a particularly strong point, offering tighter bounds and a more accurate characterization of convergence for the considered class of loss functions. The discussion of why matrix W (line 165) may not be positive semi-definite in general (lines 165-168) and the subsequent solutions (preconditioning in Sections 3.1.1 and 3.1.2) further demonstrate the depth of the analysis.

**Weaknesses:**
1.  Introduction could be more straightforward and can dive straight into the main technical contributions of the work. It was not clear why the problem is well motivated and what are the main technical hurdles until reading section 2 and onwards. A clearer presentation in the intro can make the paper much more readable and well-motivated. While the abstract (lines 1-17) effectively outlines the problem of communication pressure and memory constraints, and the role of IST, the main introduction (lines 1-68) could more directly connect these high-level motivations to the specific theoretical challenges addressed in the paper. For instance, the statement "A rigorous understanding of IST convergence virtually does not exist" (line 63) is a crucial motivation that could be brought forward and elaborated upon earlier, perhaps by outlining the specific gaps in prior theoretical work that the current study aims to fill, rather than waiting until the "Summary of contributions" subsection. This would help readers immediately grasp the significance of the paper's theoretical endeavor.

**Questions:**
1.  The authors mentioned that prior work on the convergence of IST focus "on overparameterized single hidden layer neural networks with ReLU activations" (line 60). It is not entirely clear to me why the authors considered quadratic form and what is the tradeoff between their work and prior work in this regard. A more thorough explanation would be appreciated. While the authors state that a quadratic problem "serves as a suitable problem for observing complex phenomena and providing theoretical insights, which can also be observed in practical scenarios" (lines 142-144), and reference its use in understanding neural networks, the direct link to the specific limitations of prior work on ReLU networks (e.g., [28]) could be strengthened. Does the quadratic model allow for a more general understanding of IST's core mechanisms (e.g., bias, variance due to sketching) that might be obscured by the non-convexity and specific architectural choices of ReLU networks? A brief discussion on whether the insights gained from the quadratic model are expected to transfer to more complex neural network architectures, and what challenges would arise in such a generalization, would enhance this point.

**Limitations:**
The work is theoretical and does not seem to have any potential negative societal impact. The theoretical nature of this work is well-aligned with its objective to provide a rigorous understanding of IST convergence, addressing a known gap in the literature as highlighted in the introduction (line 63). The paper acknowledges this by suggesting future research directions that involve extending the findings to more practical scenarios like cross-device federated learning and non-quadratic models (lines 307-311).

**Rating:**
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

1. Introduction could be more straightforward and can dive straight into the main technical contributions of the work. A clearer presentation in the intro can make the paper much more readable and well-motivated.

Questions:
1. The authors mentioned that prior work on the convergence of IST focus "on overparameterized single hidden layer neural networks with ReLU activations". It is not entirely clear to me why the authors considered quadratic form and what is the tradeoff between their work and prior work in this regard. A more thorough explanation would be appreciated.

Limitations:
The work is theoretical and does not seem to have any potential negative societal impact.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
